1.name: Patrick Leahy
1.alma_mater: Saint Michael's College; Georgetown University Law Center
1.biography: Patrick J. Leahy is a Democratic politician and attorney who served as U.S. Senator from Vermont (1975–2023), the first Democrat elected to the Senate from Vermont. He chaired major committees including Judiciary and Appropriations and served as Senate president pro tempore (2012–2015; 2021–2023). He is associated with the “Leahy Law” restricting U.S. aid to foreign security forces implicated in human rights abuses.

2.name: David Pryor
2.biography: David H. Pryor (1934–2024) was an Arkansas Democrat who served in the U.S. House (1966–1973), as governor of Arkansas (1975–1979), and in the U.S. Senate (1979–1997). He began his career in the Arkansas House of Representatives (1960–1966) and later led the Arkansas Democratic Party as acting chair (2008–2009) after Bill Gwatney’s assassination.

3.name: David Boren
3.alma_mater: University of Oklahoma; University of Oxford
3.biography: David Lyle Boren (1941–2025) was an Oklahoma Democrat. He served in the Oklahoma House of Representatives, then as governor of Oklahoma (1975–1979). He was U.S. Senator from Oklahoma (1979–1994), becoming a leading voice on national security and chairing the Senate Select Committee on Intelligence. After leaving the Senate, he served as president of the University of Oklahoma (1994–2018).

4.name: Howell Heflin
4.biography: Howell Thomas Heflin (1921–2005) was an Alabama lawyer, judge, and Democratic politician. He served as Chief Justice of the Alabama Supreme Court (1971–1979) before representing Alabama in the U.S. Senate (1979–1997). In the Senate he served on major committees, including Judiciary, and was known as a generally conservative Southern Democrat. He retired in 1997 and was succeeded by Jeff Sessions.

5.name: Tom Harkin
5.alma_mater: Iowa State University; The Catholic University of America (Columbus School of Law)
5.biography: Thomas Richard Harkin (born 1939) is an American lawyer and politician. A Democrat, he served as U.S. Representative for Iowa’s 5th district (1975–1985) and U.S. Senator from Iowa (1985–2015). A former U.S. Navy jet pilot (1962–1967), he later worked as a congressional aide and won election to the House in 1974, then the Senate in 1984.

6.name: Kent Conrad
6.biography: Kent Conrad (born 1948) is a North Dakota Democrat who served in the U.S. House (1981–1987) and U.S. Senate (1987–2013). He was a leading budget specialist, serving multiple terms as chair or ranking member of the Senate Budget Committee. Before Congress he worked as a state tax commissioner. He did not seek reelection in 2012 and later worked with fiscal-policy organizations.

7.name: Thomas Daschle
7.biography: Thomas A. “Tom” Daschle served as a Democratic U.S. Representative from South Dakota (1979–1987) and U.S. Senator (1987–2005). He became Senate Minority Leader in 1995 and Senate Majority Leader in 2001, leading Senate Democrats for a decade. In 2004 he narrowly lost reelection to John Thune. After Congress, he worked as a policy adviser/lobbyist and at the Center for American Progress.

8.name: Max Baucus
8.biography: Max Baucus served as a Democratic U.S. Representative from Montana (1975–1978) and U.S. Senator (1978–2014), becoming the state’s longest-serving senator. He chaired the Senate Finance Committee and played a major role in federal tax and health policy debates. After leaving the Senate, he served as U.S. Ambassador to China (2014–2017).

9.name: J. Kerrey
9.biography: Joseph Robert "Bob" Kerrey (born 1943) is a Democratic politician who served as U.S. Senator from Nebraska (1989–2001) and previously as Governor of Nebraska (1983–1987). A Vietnam War U.S. Navy SEAL, he received the Medal of Honor. He ran for the 1992 Democratic presidential nomination, later led The New School as president (2001–2010), and served in other public roles.

10.name: Russel Feingold
10.biography: Russell D. Feingold (born 1953) served as a U.S. Senator from Wisconsin (1993–2011). Previously he was a Wisconsin state senator (1983–1993) and served as a Peace Corps volunteer. In the Senate he championed campaign finance reform, co-sponsoring the Bipartisan Campaign Reform Act (McCain–Feingold), and opposed the Iraq War. After leaving office he ran unsuccessfully for Senate in 2016.

11.name: Richard Lugar
11.biography: Richard G. Lugar (1932–2019) was a Republican U.S. Senator from Indiana (1977–2013). Before the Senate, he served on the Indianapolis Board of School Commissioners and was mayor of Indianapolis (1968–1976). In the Senate he became a leading voice on foreign policy and arms control, notably the Nunn–Lugar Cooperative Threat Reduction program to secure and dismantle weapons of mass destruction in the former Soviet Union.

12.name: Robert Dole
12.biography: Robert J. "Bob" Dole (1923–2021) was a Republican from Kansas who served in the U.S. House (1961–1969) and U.S. Senate (1969–1996). A World War II Army veteran, he became Senate Republican leader, serving as Majority Leader (1985–1987, 1995–1996). He was the 1976 Republican vice-presidential nominee and the party’s 1996 presidential nominee.

13.name: Jesse Helms
13.biography: Jesse Helms (1921–2008) was a Republican U.S. Senator from North Carolina (1973–2003) and a prominent conservative activist and former journalist. He became a leading voice on foreign policy as chair of the Senate Foreign Relations Committee (1995–2001). Helms was known for strong anti-communism and outspoken positions on social issues, backing conservative candidates and movements nationally, and influencing Republican Party politics for decades.

14.name: Thad Cochran
14.biography: William Thad Cochran (1937–2019) was a Republican U.S. Senator from Mississippi (1978–2018) and previously a U.S. Representative (1973–1978). A University of Mississippi graduate and Navy veteran, he practiced law before entering Congress. He won a three-way Senate race in 1978 and was reelected repeatedly, becoming a leading appropriator and long-serving Mississippi Republican.

15.name: Mitch McConnell
15.alma_mater: University of Louisville; University of Kentucky
15.biography: Addison Mitchell McConnell III (born 1942) is a Republican U.S. Senator from Kentucky, first elected in 1984 and serving since 1985. He previously served as Jefferson County Judge/Executive in Kentucky. In the Senate he became a leading party strategist and served as Senate Republican leader from 2007 to 2025, including as majority leader (2015–2021) and minority leader (2007–2015, 2021–2025).

16.name: Larry Craig
16.biography: Lawrence E. “Larry” Craig (born 1945) is a retired Republican politician from Idaho. He served in the Idaho Senate (1974–1980), the U.S. House representing Idaho’s 1st district (1981–1991), and the U.S. Senate (1991–2009). Educated at the University of Idaho, he later ended his Senate career amid a 2007 airport restroom scandal and did not seek reelection in 2008.

17.name: Paul Coverdell
17.biography: Paul Douglas Coverdell (1939–2000) was a Republican U.S. Senator from Georgia (1993–2000). Before the Senate, he served in the Georgia Senate and the Georgia Public Service Commission. He was director of the U.S. Peace Corps from 1989 to 1991 under President George H. W. Bush. Coverdell served in the Senate until his death in 2000.

18.name: Charles Grassley
18.alma_mater: Iowa State Teachers College (now University of Northern Iowa)
18.biography: Charles E. "Chuck" Grassley (born 1933) is a Republican U.S. senator from Iowa, first elected in 1980 and serving since 1981. He previously served in the Iowa House of Representatives (1959–1975) and the U.S. House (1975–1981). Grassley has chaired the Senate Finance Committee and served as president pro tempore of the Senate (2019–2021; again beginning in 2025).

19.name: Robert Byrd
19.biography: Robert C. Byrd was a Democrat who served as U.S. Representative from West Virginia (1953–1959) and U.S. Senator (1959–2010), the longest-serving senator in U.S. history. He held major leadership roles including Senate Majority Leader, Minority Leader, and President pro tempore. Byrd earlier served in the West Virginia legislature and later repudiated his 1940s involvement with the Ku Klux Klan.

21.name: Ernest Hollings
21.alma_mater: The Citadel; University of South Carolina School of Law
21.biography: Ernest Frederick “Fritz” Hollings (1922–2019) was a South Carolina Democrat who served in the U.S. Senate from 1966 to 2005. Earlier he was in the South Carolina House (1949–1954), lieutenant governor (1955–1959), and governor (1959–1963). A longtime senator alongside Strom Thurmond, he became known for advocacy on commerce, budget, and defense issues.

22.name: J. Johnston
22.biography: John Warfield Johnston was a Virginia lawyer and politician who served in the Virginia State Senate and later as a U.S. Senator from Virginia after the state’s readmission following the Civil War. Initially barred under the Fourteenth Amendment due to Confederate service, he was ultimately seated and became the first former Confederate to serve in the U.S. Senate. He was a Democrat.

23.name: Jim Sasser
23.alma_mater: Vanderbilt University; Vanderbilt University Law School
23.biography: James Ralph "Jim" Sasser (1936–2024) was a Tennessee attorney and Democratic politician. He served in the U.S. Senate from 1977 to 1995, including as chair of the Senate Budget Committee. After losing reelection in 1994, he was appointed U.S. Ambassador to China (1996–1999) during the Clinton administration. He remains the most recent Democrat to represent Tennessee in the U.S. Senate.

24.name: Dennis DeConcini
24.biography: Dennis DeConcini (born 1937) is an Arizona lawyer and philanthropist who served as a Democratic U.S. Senator from Arizona from 1977 to 1995. He previously served as Pima County Attorney. In the Senate he was active on judiciary and intelligence matters and co-sponsored the 1982 Voting Rights Act extension. After leaving office, he worked in law and civic and charitable activities in Arizona.

25.name: Dale Bumpers
25.biography: Dale Bumpers (1925–2016) was an Arkansas lawyer and Democratic politician. He served in the U.S. Marine Corps, won the governorship of Arkansas (1971–1975), and then served as U.S. Senator from Arkansas (1975–1999). In the Senate he became a prominent voice on issues such as ethics, law, and government reform, and was later counsel at the Washington law firm Arent Fox.

26.name: Frank Lautenberg
26.biography: Frank R. Lautenberg (1924–2013) was a Democratic U.S. Senator from New Jersey (1982–2001; 2003–2013). A co-founder and longtime CEO of Automatic Data Processing (ADP), he entered politics in 1982. He returned to the Senate in 2003 after Robert Torricelli withdrew. Lautenberg became New Jersey’s longest-serving senator and was known for work on transportation safety and environmental policy.

27.name: Barbara Mikulski
27.alma_mater: Mount Saint Agnes College; University of Maryland School of Social Work
27.biography: Barbara A. Mikulski is a Democratic politician and former social worker who represented Maryland in the U.S. Senate (1987–2017) after serving in the U.S. House (1977–1987). Raised in East Baltimore, she entered politics as a community organizer and Baltimore City Council member (1971–1976). In 1986 she became the first woman elected to the U.S. Senate from Maryland.

28.name: Harry Reid
28.alma_mater: Utah State University; George Washington University
28.biography: Harry Reid was a Democratic politician from Nevada. He served as Henderson city attorney, a member of the Nevada Assembly, and lieutenant governor (1971–1975). After chairing the Nevada Gaming Commission, he represented Nevada’s 1st district in the U.S. House (1983–1987) and served as U.S. senator (1987–2017). Reid led Senate Democrats (2005–2017) and was Senate majority leader (2007–2015).

29.name: Herbert Kohl
29.biography: Herbert H. Kohl (1935–2023) was a Milwaukee businessman and Democratic politician. He ran his family’s Kohl’s department store business and bought the NBA’s Milwaukee Bucks in 1985. Kohl served as a U.S. Senator from Wisconsin from 1989 to 2013, focusing on consumer protection, government oversight, and education issues. He previously chaired the Wisconsin Democratic Party and was a major philanthropist in Wisconsin.

30.name: Patty Murray
30.biography: Patty Murray (born 1950) is a Democratic politician serving as U.S. Senator from Washington since 1993, the state’s first female senator. Before the Senate, she served on a local school board and in the Washington State Senate (1989–1993). She has been a leading voice on education, health care, transportation, and appropriations, and served as Senate president pro tempore from 2023 to 2025.

31.name: Dianne Feinstein
31.biography: Dianne Feinstein (1933–2023) was a Democratic politician who served as U.S. senator from California from 1992 until her death in 2023. A Stanford graduate, she was elected to the San Francisco Board of Supervisors and became its first female president. She became mayor of San Francisco in 1978 after Mayor George Moscone’s assassination and served until 1988.

32.name: Mark Hatfield
32.biography: Mark O. Hatfield (1922–2011) was a moderate Republican from Oregon. After U.S. Navy service in World War II, he taught at Willamette University and served in Oregon’s legislature, then as secretary of state and two-term governor. He represented Oregon in the U.S. Senate from 1967 to 1997, becoming a senior appropriator and chairing the Senate Appropriations Committee, known for independence and opposition to the Vietnam War.

33.name: Ted Stevens
33.biography: Theodore F. Stevens was a Republican U.S. Senator from Alaska (1968–2009). A World War II pilot and lawyer, he served as U.S. Attorney in Fairbanks and later as Solicitor of the U.S. Department of the Interior. In the Senate he became a powerful appropriator, chaired key committees, and served as president pro tempore (2003–2007). He was long Alaska’s senior senator.

34.name: Alfonso D'Amato
34.alma_mater: Syracuse University; Washington and Lee University School of Law
34.biography: Alfonse M. D'Amato served as a Republican U.S. Senator from New York (1981–1999). He previously was a Hempstead, New York town supervisor and a U.S. representative (1977–1981). In the Senate he chaired the Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs Committee and later the Special Committee on Aging. He lost reelection in 1998 to Democrat Chuck Schumer.

35.name: Arlen Specter
35.alma_mater: University of Pennsylvania; Yale Law School
35.biography: Arlen Specter (1930–2012) was a lawyer and longtime U.S. Senator from Pennsylvania (1981–2011). First elected in 1980 as a Republican, he became the state’s longest-serving senator and later switched to the Democratic Party in 2009. He previously served as Philadelphia district attorney and as assistant counsel to the Warren Commission, associated with the “single-bullet theory.”

36.name: Pete Domenici
36.biography: Pete Domenici (1932–2017) was a Republican U.S. Senator from New Mexico, serving from 1973 to 2009. A former Albuquerque city commissioner, he became the state’s longest-serving senator. He chaired major committees including the Senate Budget Committee and the Senate Energy and Natural Resources Committee, and was influential on federal budgeting, energy policy, and infrastructure issues.

37.name: Don Nickles
37.biography: Donald Lee Nickles (born 1948) served as a Republican U.S. senator from Oklahoma from 1981 to 2005. A fiscal and social conservative, he rose to Senate leadership as Whip and chaired the Senate Budget Committee. After retiring, he founded the Nickles Group, a lobbying firm.

38.name: Phil Gramm
38.biography: Phil Gramm is an American economist and politician. He served in the U.S. House from Texas (1979–1985), then as a U.S. Senator from Texas (1985–2002). Elected initially as a Democrat, he switched to the Republican Party in 1983 and became a leading conservative voice on fiscal and banking issues, chairing the Senate Banking Committee. He sought the 1996 Republican presidential nomination.

39.name: Christopher Bond
39.alma_mater: Princeton University; University of Virginia School of Law
39.biography: Christopher “Kit” Bond was a Republican politician from Missouri. He served as Missouri state auditor (1971–1973) and as governor in two nonconsecutive terms (1973–1977, 1981–1985). Elected to the U.S. Senate in 1986, he represented Missouri from 1987 to 2011, winning reelection three times before retiring in 2011 and being succeeded by Roy Blunt.

40.name: Slade Gorton
40.biography: Thomas Slade Gorton III was a Republican U.S. senator from Washington, serving 1981–1987 and 1989–2001. A lawyer and WWII/Korean War veteran, he previously served as Washington attorney general and as a member of the state legislature. He held both of Washington’s Senate seats at different times and lost close reelection bids in 1986 and 2000 (after a recount).

41.name: Connie Mack
41.biography: Connie Mack III (Cornelius Alexander McGillicuddy III) served as a Republican U.S. Representative from Florida (1983–1989) and as U.S. Senator from Florida (1989–2001). In the Senate he became chairman of the Senate Republican Conference (1997–2001). He was a prominent conservative voice and was considered for the Republican vice-presidential nomination in 1996 and 2000.

42.name: Conrad Burns
42.biography: Conrad Ray Burns (1935–2016) was a Republican politician who served as U.S. Senator from Montana (1989–2007) after serving in the Montana Senate. A rancher and businessman, he became Montana’s longest-serving Republican senator. In the Senate he served on Appropriations and chaired the Interior Subcommittee, and led telecommunications-related work on the Commerce Committee. After leaving office, he worked as a lobbyist.

43.name: Sam Nunn
43.alma_mater: Emory University; University of Virginia School of Law
43.biography: Samuel Augustus Nunn Jr. (born 1938) served as a Democratic U.S. senator from Georgia (1972–1997). A leading voice on national security, he chaired the Senate Armed Services Committee and helped shape major defense reforms, including the Goldwater–Nichols Act. He co-sponsored the Nunn–Lugar Cooperative Threat Reduction program to secure and dismantle former Soviet weapons of mass destruction. After leaving the Senate, he co-founded the Nuclear Threat Initiative.

44.name: J. Exon
44.biography: John James Exon (1921–2005) was a Nebraska businessman and Democratic politician. He served as the 33rd governor of Nebraska (1971–1979) and as U.S. senator from Nebraska (1979–1997), winning election in 1978 and reelection in 1984 and 1990. He was noted as a moderate Democrat and the only Democrat to hold Nebraska’s Class 2 Senate seat.

45.name: Carl Levin
45.biography: Carl Levin (1934–2021) was a Democratic U.S. senator from Michigan (1979–2015). A Detroit native, he served on the Detroit City Council (1969–1977), including as council president, before defeating Republican Sen. Robert P. Griffin in 1978. In the Senate he focused on national security and oversight, chairing the Senate Armed Services Committee (2001–2003, 2007–2015).

46.name: Edward Kennedy
46.alma_mater: Harvard University; University of Virginia School of Law
46.biography: Edward M. "Ted" Kennedy served as U.S. Senator from Massachusetts (1962–2009). A leading liberal Democrat, he chaired the Senate Health, Education, Labor and Pensions Committee and earlier led the Judiciary Committee. He was a central figure in major legislation on health care, labor, immigration, civil rights, and education, including the ADA and CHIP. He ran for the 1980 Democratic presidential nomination.

47.name: Jeff Bingaman
47.biography: Jeff Bingaman is a retired American politician who served as U.S. senator from New Mexico (1983–2013). A Democrat, he previously served as New Mexico attorney general (1979–1983). In the Senate he was a longtime leader on energy policy, chairing the Senate Energy and Natural Resources Committee, and served on committees including Finance and Armed Services. After leaving office, he became a fellow at Stanford Law School.

48.name: John Glenn
48.biography: John Herschel Glenn Jr. was a U.S. Senator from Ohio (1974–1999) and a Democrat. Before politics he was a Marine Corps aviator and NASA astronaut, becoming the first American to orbit Earth in 1962. He later returned to space in 1998 at age 77. Glenn previously ran for president in 1984 and was known for work on government affairs and science-related issues.

49.name: Richard Shelby
49.alma_mater: University of Alabama; University of Alabama School of Law
49.biography: Richard C. “Dick” Shelby (born 1934) served as U.S. senator from Alabama from 1987 to 2023. Elected in 1986 as a Democrat, he switched to the Republican Party in 1994. Earlier he served in the Alabama State Senate (1970–1978) and in the U.S. House from Alabama (1979–1987), and worked as a prosecutor and federal magistrate.

50.name: Bob Graham
50.biography: Bob Graham (1936–2024) was a Florida Democrat who served as governor of Florida (1979–1987) and U.S. senator (1987–2005). In the Senate he focused on intelligence and national security and chaired the Senate Select Committee on Intelligence (2001–2003), including during the 9/11 and Iraq War period. Earlier he was a Florida state legislator. He ran for the 2004 Democratic presidential nomination.

51.name: Charles Robb
51.biography: Charles “Chuck” Robb (born 1939) is a Democratic politician and former U.S. Marine Corps officer. He served as governor of Virginia (1982–1986) and as U.S. senator from Virginia (1989–2001), losing reelection in 2000 to George Allen. He is married to Lynda Bird Johnson, daughter of President Lyndon B. Johnson. After the Senate, he co-chaired the Iraq Intelligence Commission.

52.name: Joseph Lieberman
52.biography: Joseph I. Lieberman (1942–2024) was a lawyer and politician who served as U.S. Senator from Connecticut (1989–2013). Previously he served in the Connecticut Senate and as Connecticut attorney general (1983–1989). Elected to the Senate in 1988 and re-elected in 1994, 2000, and 2006, he ran as an Independent in 2006 and caucused with Democrats. He was the 2000 Democratic vice presidential nominee.

53.name: Strom Thurmond
53.alma_mater: Clemson Agricultural College (now Clemson University), University of South Carolina
53.biography: James Strom Thurmond Sr. (1902–2003) served as U.S. Senator from South Carolina (1954–2003), after serving as governor (1947–1951). Initially a Democrat, he ran for president in 1948 as the Dixiecrat nominee and switched to the Republican Party in 1964. He was a leading conservative and a prominent opponent of civil rights legislation, including a record 24-hour, 18-minute filibuster against the Civil Rights Act of 1957.

54.name: John Warner
54.alma_mater: Washington and Lee University; University of Virginia School of Law
54.biography: John William Warner III (1927–2021) was a Republican U.S. Senator from Virginia (1979–2009). A World War II and Korean War veteran and lawyer, he served as U.S. Secretary of the Navy (1972–1974) before entering the Senate. He chaired the Senate Rules Committee (1995–1999) and the Senate Armed Services Committee (1999–2001, 2003–2007). He retired in 2009.

55.name: William Cohen
55.alma_mater: Bowdoin College; Boston University School of Law
55.biography: William S. Cohen is a Maine Republican who served in the U.S. House (1973–1979) and U.S. Senate (1979–1997). In the Senate he became known as a moderate and served on major national security and government affairs issues. After leaving Congress, he was Secretary of Defense (1997–2001) under President Bill Clinton and later worked in consulting and writing.

56.name: John McCain
56.biography: John Sidney McCain III (1936–2018) was a Republican who served in Congress for over 35 years: U.S. Representative from Arizona (1983–1987) and U.S. Senator from Arizona (1987–2018). A U.S. Navy aviator, he was shot down in Vietnam and held as a prisoner of war from 1967 to 1973. He was the 2008 Republican presidential nominee.

57.name: Trent Lott
57.biography: Chester Trent Lott Sr. served as a Republican U.S. Representative from Mississippi (1973–1989) and U.S. Senator (1989–2007). A prominent Senate leader, he served as Senate Majority Leader (1996–2001, 2002–2003) and Senate Minority Leader (2001–2002). He resigned as Majority Leader in 2002 after controversy over remarks praising Strom Thurmond’s 1948 campaign, and later became a lobbyist.

58.name: Dan Coats
58.alma_mater: Wheaton College; Indiana University School of Law – Indianapolis
58.biography: Daniel Ray Coats (born 1943) is an Indiana Republican politician. He served in the U.S. Army (1966–1968), represented Indiana’s 4th district in the U.S. House (1981–1989), and was appointed to the U.S. Senate in 1989, then elected in 1990 and 1992, serving until 1999. He later served as U.S. Ambassador to Germany and Director of National Intelligence.

59.name: Robert Smith
59.biography: Robert C. Smith (born 1931) is a Republican politician who served as U.S. Senator from New Hampshire (1990–2003), appointed to fill the seat of Gordon J. Humphrey and later elected to a full term. Before the Senate he was New Hampshire attorney general (1984–1990) and earlier served in state government. He left the Senate in 2003 and returned to law and public affairs.

60.name: Dirk Kempthorne
60.alma_mater: San Diego State University; University of Idaho
60.biography: Dirk Kempthorne (born 1951) is an Idaho Republican politician. He served as mayor of Boise (1986–1993), then as U.S. senator from Idaho (1993–1999). He was elected governor of Idaho (1999–2006) and later served as U.S. secretary of the interior (2006–2009) under President George W. Bush. After leaving office, he led the American Council of Life Insurers.

61.name: Lauch Faircloth
61.biography: Duncan McLauchlin “Lauch” Faircloth (1928–2023) was a North Carolina politician and businessman. Long active as a Democrat, he served as chair of the North Carolina Highway Commission (1969–1973) and as North Carolina Secretary of Commerce. He ran unsuccessfully for governor in 1984, switched to the Republican Party in 1991, and served one term as U.S. senator from North Carolina (1993–1999) after defeating Terry Sanford.

62.name: Kay Hutchison
62.biography: Kay Bailey Hutchison (born 1943) is a Republican politician and attorney who served as U.S. Senator from Texas (1993–2013), becoming the first woman to represent Texas in the Senate. Earlier she served in the Texas House (1972–1976) and as Texas State Treasurer (1991–1993). She sought the GOP nomination for Texas governor in 2010 and later served as U.S. Ambassador to NATO (2017–2021).

63.name: Donald Riegle
63.biography: Donald Wayne Riegle Jr. is a Michigan politician who served in Congress first as a U.S. Representative (1967–1976) and then as a U.S. Senator (1976–1995). A Democrat during his Senate career, he became a prominent figure in banking and consumer issues and chaired the Senate Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs. He did not seek reelection in 1994.

64.name: Paul Sarbanes
64.alma_mater: Princeton University; Balliol College, Oxford; Harvard Law School
64.biography: Paul Sarbanes (1933–2020) was a Maryland Democrat who served in the U.S. House (1971–1977) and U.S. Senate (1977–2007). Before Congress he served in the Maryland House of Delegates (1967–1971). A lawyer and one of the first Greek American senators, he was known for work on banking and financial regulation, including co-sponsoring the Sarbanes–Oxley Act.

65.name: Christopher Dodd
65.alma_mater: Providence College; University of Louisville School of Law
65.biography: Christopher John “Chris” Dodd served as a Democratic U.S. Representative from Connecticut’s 2nd district (1975–1981) and U.S. Senator from Connecticut (1981–2011). A Connecticut native, he is the son of former Senator Thomas J. Dodd. Before politics he served in the Peace Corps and the Army Reserve while in law school. After leaving the Senate he worked as a lobbyist.

66.name: John Kerry
66.alma_mater: Yale University; Boston College Law School
66.biography: John Forbes Kerry served as U.S. Senator from Massachusetts (1985–2013) after serving as the state’s lieutenant governor (1983–1985). A Vietnam War Navy veteran and former prosecutor, he chaired the Senate Foreign Relations Committee and was the Democratic nominee for president in 2004. He later served as U.S. secretary of state (2013–2017) and as special presidential envoy for climate (2021–2024).

67.name: Richard Bryan
67.alma_mater: University of Nevada, Reno; University of California, Hastings College of the Law
67.biography: Richard Hudson Bryan (born 1937) is a Nevada attorney and Democratic politician. He served in the Nevada State Senate, was Nevada attorney general, and was the 25th governor of Nevada (1983–1989). Bryan represented Nevada in the U.S. Senate from 1989 to 2001 before retiring from elective office.

68.name: Barbara Boxer
68.biography: Barbara Boxer is a Democratic politician who served as U.S. Senator from California (1993–2017) and previously represented California’s 6th congressional district in the U.S. House (1983–1993). Before Congress, she served on the Marin County Board of Supervisors and worked as a journalist and congressional aide. Born in Brooklyn, New York, she earned a bachelor’s degree from Brooklyn College.

69.name: Ben Campbell
69.biography: Ben Nighthorse Campbell (1933–2025) served Colorado in Congress, first as U.S. Representative for Colorado’s 3rd district (1987–1993) and then as U.S. Senator (1993–2005). A U.S. Air Force veteran and member of the Northern Cheyenne, he was for a time the only Native American in Congress. Elected as a Democrat, he switched to the Republican Party in 1995 and retired in 2005.

70.name: Carol Moseley-Braun
70.alma_mater: University of Illinois at Chicago; University of Chicago
70.biography: Carol Moseley-Braun is a Democratic politician, lawyer, and diplomat. She served as U.S. Senator from Illinois (1993–1999), becoming the first African-American woman elected to the Senate and the first female U.S. senator from Illinois. Earlier she served in the Illinois House (1979–1988) and as Cook County Recorder of Deeds (1988–1992). After losing reelection in 1998, she was U.S. Ambassador to New Zealand and Samoa (1999–2001).

71.name: Robert Bennet
71.alma_mater: Brigham Young University; University of Utah
71.biography: Robert Foster Bennett (1933–2016) was a Republican U.S. Senator from Utah (1993–2011). He previously served as staff secretary to President Richard Nixon and later worked in business, including leading Franklin Quest (FranklinCovey). In the Senate he focused on budget, finance, and intelligence matters. Bennett lost the 2010 Republican convention nomination after opposition from Tea Party-aligned activists.

72.name: William Roth
72.biography: William V. Roth Jr. (1921–2003) was a Republican politician from Wilmington, Delaware. A World War II veteran and lawyer, he served as Delaware’s at-large U.S. Representative (1967–1970) and then as U.S. Senator (1971–2001). He was known for tax and retirement policy and sponsored legislation creating the Roth IRA. He was the last Republican to serve as U.S. Senator from Delaware.

73.name: Paul Simon
73.biography: Paul Simon (1928–2003) was an Illinois Democrat known for his bow tie and public-service advocacy. He served in the Illinois legislature, then represented Illinois in the U.S. House (1975–1985) and U.S. Senate (1985–1997). He sought the 1988 Democratic presidential nomination. After leaving the Senate, he founded the Public Policy Institute at Southern Illinois University Carbondale and taught there.

74.name: Hank Brown
74.biography: George Hanks "Hank" Brown is a Colorado Republican politician and lawyer. He served in the U.S. House representing Colorado’s 4th district (1973–1981) and later briefly served as U.S. Senator from Colorado (1991). After leaving Congress, he held leadership roles in higher education, including president of the University of Northern Colorado (1998–2002) and president of the University of Colorado system (2005–2008).

75.name: Judd Gregg
75.alma_mater: Columbia University; Georgetown University Law Center
75.biography: Judd Alan Gregg is a Republican politician and attorney from New Hampshire. He served in the U.S. House (1981–1989), was governor of New Hampshire (1989–1993), and U.S. senator (1993–2011). In the Senate he chaired the Budget Committee and the Health, Education, Labor and Pensions Committee. He was nominated as Secretary of Commerce in 2009 but withdrew.

76.name: Wendell Ford
76.biography: Wendell H. Ford (1924–2015) was a Kentucky Democrat who served as governor (1971–1974) and then U.S. senator (1974–1999). In the Senate he became a leading Kentucky Democrat and served as Senate Democratic whip from 1991 to 1999. A World War II veteran, he was long influential in transportation and communications policy before retiring in 1999.

77.name: John Rockefeller
77.biography: John Davison “Jay” Rockefeller IV (born 1937) is a Democratic politician who served as U.S. Senator from West Virginia (1985–2015). He previously served in the West Virginia House of Delegates (1967–1968), as West Virginia Secretary of State (1969–1973), and as Governor of West Virginia (1977–1985). A member of the Rockefeller family, he entered public service in West Virginia after working with VISTA.

78.name: Lloyd Bentsen
78.biography: Lloyd M. Bentsen Jr. (1921–2006) was a Texas Democrat who served in the U.S. House (1948–1955) and as U.S. senator (1971–1993). A WWII Army Air Forces veteran, he later chaired the Senate Finance Committee (1987–1993). Bentsen was the 1988 Democratic vice-presidential nominee and served as U.S. secretary of the treasury under President Bill Clinton (1993–1994).

79.name: John Breaux
79.alma_mater: University of Louisiana at Lafayette; Louisiana State University
79.biography: John Breaux (born 1944) is a Louisiana Democrat who served in the U.S. House of Representatives (1972–1987) and the U.S. Senate (1987–2005). Often described as a conservative or centrist Southern Democrat, he joined the New Democrat Coalition and worked on major fiscal and health policy issues. After leaving Congress, he became a lobbyist and co-founded the Breaux-Lott Leadership Group.

80.name: Byron Dorgan
80.biography: Byron L. Dorgan served as U.S. Representative for North Dakota at-large (1981–1992) and U.S. Senator (1992–2011). Previously he was North Dakota Tax Commissioner and state labor commissioner. In the Senate he held leadership roles, chaired the Democratic Policy Committee, and later chaired the Senate Indian Affairs Committee. He did not seek reelection in 2010 and later worked as a policy advisor and energy-policy fellow.

81.name: John Danforth
81.biography: John C. Danforth (born 1936) is a Republican politician who served as Missouri attorney general (1969–1976) and U.S. senator from Missouri (1976–1995). Trained as an attorney, he later served as special counsel to the U.S. Department of Justice (1999–2000) and as U.S. ambassador to the United Nations (2004–2005).

82.name: Bob Packwood
82.biography: Robert W. "Bob" Packwood (born 1932) is an Oregon Republican who served in the Oregon House (1963–1969) and the U.S. Senate (1969–1995). In the Senate he became known for work on tax and budget issues and for generally moderate-to-liberal positions on some social issues. He resigned in 1995 amid allegations of sexual harassment and misconduct and an ethics investigation.

83.name: Larry Pressler
83.alma_mater: University of South Dakota; George Washington University; Harvard University
83.biography: Larry Pressler (born 1942) is a South Dakota politician who served as a Republican U.S. Representative (1975–1979) and U.S. Senator (1979–1997). A Vietnam War veteran, he became known for defense and foreign policy interests and briefly sought the 1980 Republican presidential nomination. He lost reelection in 1996 and later ran unsuccessfully for the Senate as an independent in 2014.

84.name: Bob Krueger
84.alma_mater: Texas Lutheran College; Duke University; Harvard University; University of Texas at Austin
84.biography: Robert C. "Bob" Krueger (1935–2022) was a Texas Democrat who served in the U.S. House (1975–1979) and briefly in the U.S. Senate (1993), appointed to fill Lloyd Bentsen’s vacancy. He later was U.S. Ambassador to Burundi (1994–1996). Earlier he was a political science professor and ran for the Senate several times, including losing the 1994 Democratic primary to Kay Bailey Hutchison.

85.name: Bill Bradley
85.biography: William Warren "Bill" Bradley (born 1943) is a Democrat who served as U.S. Senator from New Jersey (1979–1997). Before politics he was an Olympic gold medalist (1964) and NBA player with the New York Knicks. In the Senate he worked on tax reform and social policy. After leaving office he became an author and speaker and ran for the 2000 Democratic presidential nomination, losing to Al Gore.

87.name: Paul Wellstone
87.alma_mater: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
87.biography: Paul Wellstone (1944–2002) was a Democratic–Farmer–Labor U.S. Senator from Minnesota, serving from 1991 until his death. A former Carleton College political science professor and community organizer, he became a prominent progressive and populist voice in the Senate. Wellstone was killed in an airplane crash near Eveleth, Minnesota, in October 2002, shortly before his reelection campaign.

88.name: Harlan Mathews
88.biography: Harlan Mathews (1927–2014) was a Tennessee Democratic politician and served as an appointed interim U.S. Senator from Tennessee from 1993 to 1994. He spent more than four decades in Tennessee state government, working in both executive and legislative roles beginning in 1950, before his brief tenure in the U.S. Senate.

89.name: Malcolm Wallop
89.biography: Malcolm Wallop was a Republican U.S. Senator from Wyoming, serving three terms from 1977 to 1995. A rancher and politician, he previously served three terms in the Wyoming Legislature. In the Senate he focused on western, energy, and natural-resource issues and held various committee assignments. He was succeeded in the Senate by Craig L. Thomas.

90.name: Frank Murkowski
90.biography: Frank Murkowski is a Republican politician who served as U.S. senator from Alaska from 1981 to 2002, winning elections in 1980, 1986, 1992, and 1998. He resigned from the Senate in 2002 after being elected governor of Alaska, serving from 2002 to 2006. Upon resigning, he appointed his daughter, Lisa Murkowski, to his Senate seat.

91.name: Daniel Moynihan
91.alma_mater: City College of New York; Tufts University; Fletcher School of Law and Diplomacy
91.biography: Daniel Patrick Moynihan (1927–2003) was a Democratic U.S. Senator from New York (1977–2001). A social scientist, he served in the Kennedy and Johnson administrations, including as Assistant Secretary of Labor, and authored the 1965 Moynihan Report. He later advised President Nixon and served as U.S. Ambassador to India and U.S. Ambassador to the United Nations before his Senate career.

92.name: George Mitchell
92.alma_mater: Bowdoin College, Georgetown University Law Center
92.biography: George J. Mitchell served as U.S. Senator from Maine (1980–1995), becoming Senate Majority Leader (1989–1995). Previously he was U.S. Attorney for Maine and served as a federal judge on the U.S. District Court for Maine. After leaving the Senate, he led Northern Ireland peace talks culminating in the 1998 Good Friday Agreement and later served as U.S. Special Envoy for Middle East Peace.

93.name: Howard Metzenbaum
93.biography: Howard Metzenbaum was an American businessman and Democratic politician. He served in the Ohio General Assembly (1943–1951) and later as a U.S. Senator from Ohio, first appointed in 1974 and then elected in 1976, serving until 1995. Known as a liberal advocate for consumer protection, labor, and civil liberties, he was a prominent voice in Senate debates and investigations.

94.name: Harris Wofford
94.alma_mater: University of Chicago; Yale Law School
94.biography: Harris Wofford was a Democratic U.S. Senator from Pennsylvania (1991–1995), elected in a 1991 special election. An attorney and civil rights advocate, he served in the Kennedy and Johnson administrations, helped launch the Peace Corps, and later was president of Bryn Mawr College (1970–1978). He chaired the Pennsylvania Democratic Party and was Pennsylvania Secretary of Labor and Industry (1987–1991).

95.name: John Chafee
95.biography: John Lester Hubbard Chafee (1922–1999) was a Rhode Island Republican. A U.S. Marine Corps officer and veteran of World War II and Korea, he served in the Rhode Island legislature, as mayor of Warwick, and as the 66th governor of Rhode Island. He was Secretary of the Navy (1969–1972) and U.S. Senator from Rhode Island (1976–1999), known as a pragmatic, moderate Republican.

96.name: Alan Simpson
96.biography: Alan K. Simpson was a Republican U.S. Senator from Wyoming (1979–1997). He previously served in the Wyoming House of Representatives (1965–1977). In the Senate, he was Republican whip from 1985 to 1995, including majority whip from 1985 to 1987. After retiring, he worked in law and academia and later co-chaired the Simpson–Bowles fiscal responsibility commission.

97.name: Dave Durenberger
97.party: Republican (served in Congress 1978-1995; left party in 2005)
97.alma_mater: St. John's University; University of Minnesota Law School
97.biography: David “Dave” Durenberger (1934–2023) was a Minnesota attorney and Republican who served in the U.S. Senate from 1978 to 1995. He was elected in a 1978 special election after Hubert Humphrey’s death and won reelection in 1982 and 1988. He later became a critic of the Republican Party and formally left it in 2005.

98.name: Orrin Hatch
98.alma_mater: Brigham Young University; University of Pittsburgh
98.biography: Orrin Grant Hatch (1934–2022) was a Republican U.S. senator from Utah (1977–2019), one of the longest-serving senators in U.S. history. He chaired the Senate Judiciary Committee (1995–2001; 2003–2005) and the Senate Finance Committee (2015–2019), and earlier led the Health, Education, Labor and Pensions Committee. He served as Senate president pro tempore (2015–2019).

99.name: Claiborne Pell
99.biography: Claiborne Pell (1918–2009) was a Democrat who served as U.S. Senator from Rhode Island from 1961 to 1997, the state’s longest-serving senator. He chaired the Senate Foreign Relations Committee and championed arts, education, and public broadcasting. Pell sponsored legislation reforming the Basic Educational Opportunity Grant; it was renamed the Pell Grant in 1980. He also served in the U.S. Coast Guard during World War II.

100.name: Joseph Biden
100.alma_mater: University of Delaware; Syracuse University College of Law
100.biography: Joseph R. Biden Jr. (born 1942) is a Democrat who served as U.S. Senator from Delaware (1973–2009), chairing the Senate Judiciary and Foreign Relations Committees. He previously served on the New Castle County Council. He was vice president of the United States (2009–2017) under Barack Obama and later served as the 46th U.S. president (2021–2025).

101.name: Nancy Kassebaum
101.biography: Nancy Kassebaum Baker (born 1932) is a retired Republican politician from Kansas who served as U.S. Senator from 1978 to 1997. Daughter of 1936 GOP presidential nominee Alf Landon, she became the first woman to represent Kansas in the Senate and was briefly the chamber’s only woman. Known as a moderate, she chaired Foreign Relations’ Africa subcommittee and later the Senate Labor and Human Resources Committee.

102.name: James Jeffords
102.biography: James Merrill Jeffords was a Vermont lawyer and politician. He served in the Vermont Senate (1967–1969) and as Vermont attorney general (1969–1973). Elected to the U.S. House (1975–1989), he then served as U.S. senator from Vermont (1989–2007). In 2001 he left the Republican Party, became an independent, and caucused with Democrats.

103.name: Rick Santorum
103.alma_mater: Penn State University; University of Pittsburgh
103.biography: Rick Santorum is a Republican politician who served as U.S. Senator from Pennsylvania from 1995 to 2007, after serving in the U.S. House (1991–1995). A leading social conservative, he was the Senate Republican Conference’s third-ranking leader in his final years. He lost reelection in 2006 to Bob Casey Jr. and later ran for the 2012 Republican presidential nomination.

104.name: Ben Campbell
104.biography: Ben Nighthorse Campbell (1933–2025) was a U.S. Air Force veteran and Native American leader who served Colorado in Congress. He was U.S. Representative for Colorado’s 3rd district (1987–1993) and U.S. Senator (1993–2005). Elected as a Democrat, he switched to the Republican Party in 1995 and won reelection in 1998. He was long the only Native American in Congress.

105.name: Richard Shelby
105.alma_mater: University of Alabama; University of Alabama School of Law
105.biography: Richard C. "Dick" Shelby (born 1934) served as U.S. Senator from Alabama from 1987 to 2023. Elected to the Senate as a Democrat in 1986, he switched to the Republican Party in 1994. Earlier, he was a Tuscaloosa city prosecutor, served in the Alabama State Senate, and represented Alabama’s 7th district in the U.S. House (1979–1987).

106.name: James Inhofe
106.biography: James "Jim" Inhofe (1934–2024) was a Republican U.S. senator from Oklahoma (1994–2023), the state’s longest-serving senator. He previously served in the U.S. House (1987–1994), as mayor of Tulsa (1978–1984), and in the Oklahoma Senate and House. Inhofe was known for strong conservative positions, especially on national security and skepticism of climate-change policy.

107.name: Sheila Frahm
107.biography: Sheila Frahm (born 1945) is a Kansas Republican politician who briefly served in the U.S. Senate in 1996. She was appointed to the seat after Senator Bob Dole resigned to focus on his presidential campaign. Frahm, then Kansas lieutenant governor, ran in the 1996 special election but lost the Republican primary to Representative Sam Brownback, who won the seat.

108.name: Rod Grams
108.biography: Rod Grams (1948–2013) was a Minnesota Republican and former television news anchor. After working as an anchor at KMSP-TV in the Twin Cities, he entered politics and won election to the U.S. House in 1992, representing Minnesota’s 6th district (1993–1995). He then served as U.S. Senator from Minnesota (1995–2001), losing reelection to Mark Dayton in 2000.

109.name: William Frist
109.biography: William H. "Bill" Frist (born 1952) is a physician and Republican politician who served as U.S. Senator from Tennessee (1995–2007). A cardiothoracic transplant surgeon and founder of the Vanderbilt Transplant Center, he entered politics by defeating Sen. Jim Sasser in 1994. He chaired the National Republican Senatorial Committee and became Senate Majority Leader (2003–2007), advancing major parts of President George W. Bush’s agenda.

110.name: Olympia Snowe
110.biography: Olympia J. Snowe is a Maine Republican who served in Congress for decades, first in the U.S. House (1979–1995) and then as U.S. Senator from Maine (1995–2013). Known as one of the Senate’s most moderate Republicans, she often played a pivotal role in close votes and bipartisan negotiations. She declined to seek reelection in 2012 and retired in 2013.

111.name: Spencer Abraham
111.biography: Edward Spencer Abraham (born 1952) is an American attorney and Republican politician. He served as U.S. Senator from Michigan from 1995 to 2001, after leading the Michigan Republican Party. After leaving the Senate, he was U.S. Secretary of Energy under President George W. Bush (2001–2005). He helped found the Federalist Society and co-founded the Harvard Journal of Law & Public Policy.

112.name: Ron Wyden
112.alma_mater: University of California, Santa Barbara; Stanford University; University of Oregon School of Law
112.biography: Ron Wyden (born 1949) is a Democrat serving as U.S. senator from Oregon since 1996. He previously represented Oregon in the U.S. House (1981–1996) before winning a special Senate election. Wyden is known for civil-liberties and privacy advocacy, including internet freedom and limits on government surveillance. He has chaired and served as ranking member of the Senate Finance Committee.

113.name: John Ashcroft
113.alma_mater: Yale University; University of Chicago Law School
113.biography: John Ashcroft (born 1942) is a Missouri Republican who served in the U.S. Senate (1995–2001). He previously was Missouri state auditor (1973–1975), attorney general (1976–1985), and governor (1985–1993). After leaving the Senate, he became U.S. attorney general under President George W. Bush (2001–2005), then worked as a lobbyist and founded The Ashcroft Group.

114.name: Craig Thomas
114.biography: Craig Lyle Thomas was a Republican politician who served as U.S. Senator from Wyoming (1995–2007). Previously he represented Wyoming’s at-large district in the U.S. House (1989–1995) after winning a 1989 special election. Before Congress he worked in agriculture and state agencies, was vice president of the Wyoming Farm Bureau, and served in the Wyoming House of Representatives (1985–1989).

115.name: Jon Kyl
115.biography: Jon Llewellyn Kyl (born 1942) is a Republican politician and lobbyist. He served in the U.S. House representing Arizona (1987–1995) and as U.S. Senator from Arizona (1995–2013). Kyl was Senate Minority Whip (2007–2013). After John McCain’s death, he briefly returned to the Senate in 2018, resigning in 2019. He later worked as a lobbyist at Covington & Burling.

116.name: Fred Thompson
116.biography: Fred Dalton Thompson (1942–2015) was a Republican U.S. Senator from Tennessee (1994–2003), appointed to fill a vacancy and later elected to a full term. A lawyer and former U.S. Attorney, he gained national visibility as counsel during the Senate Watergate investigation. After leaving the Senate, he worked as a lobbyist, radio host, and actor, and ran for the 2008 Republican presidential nomination.

117.name: Michael DeWine
117.biography: Richard Michael (Mike) DeWine (born 1947) is an Ohio Republican politician and attorney. He served in the U.S. House (1983–1991) and as U.S. Senator from Ohio (1995–2007). He was Ohio lieutenant governor (1991–1995), Ohio attorney general (2011–2019), and became governor of Ohio in 2019.

118.name: Arlen Specter
118.alma_mater: University of Pennsylvania; Yale Law School
118.biography: Arlen Specter (1930–2012) was a lawyer and long-serving U.S. Senator from Pennsylvania (1981–2011). He previously served as Philadelphia District Attorney (1966–1974) and as assistant counsel to the Warren Commission. Elected to the Senate as a Republican in 1980, he became known as a moderate and switched to the Democratic Party in 2009. He lost the 2010 Democratic primary.

119.name: Richard Lugar
119.biography: Richard G. Lugar (1932–2019) was a Republican U.S. Senator from Indiana (1977–2013). Earlier he served on the Indianapolis Board of School Commissioners and was mayor of Indianapolis (1968–1976). In the Senate he became a leading voice on foreign policy and arms control, notably coauthoring the Nunn–Lugar Cooperative Threat Reduction program to secure and dismantle weapons of mass destruction after the Cold War.

120.name: Michael DeWine
120.alma_mater: Miami University; Ohio Northern University College of Law
120.biography: Richard Michael DeWine (born 1947) is an Ohio politician and attorney. He served in the Ohio Senate, then as U.S. Representative from Ohio (1983–1991) and U.S. Senator from Ohio (1995–2007). He was Ohio’s lieutenant governor (1991–1995) and attorney general (2011–2019), and became governor of Ohio in 2019. He is a Republican.

121.name: Jon Kyl
121.biography: Jon Kyl (born 1942) is an American politician and lobbyist. A Republican, he served as U.S. Representative from Arizona (1987–1995) and U.S. Senator from Arizona (1995–2013), including as Senate Minority Whip (2007–2013). After John McCain’s death, he briefly returned to the Senate in 2018. He later worked as a lobbyist at Covington & Burling.

122.name: James Inhofe
122.biography: James “Jim” Inhofe (1934–2024) was a Republican politician who served as U.S. senator from Oklahoma (1994–2023), becoming the state’s longest-serving senator. He previously served as U.S. representative from Oklahoma (1987–1994) and held multiple state and local offices. Known for strong conservative positions, he was influential on defense and environmental policy, including chairing the Senate Environment and Public Works Committee.

123.name: Kay Hutchison
123.biography: Kay Bailey Hutchison (born 1943) served as a Republican U.S. Senator from Texas (1993–2013), elected in a 1993 special election and re-elected in 1994, 2000, and 2006. Earlier she served in the Texas House (1972–1976) and as Texas State Treasurer (1991–1993). She later became U.S. Permanent Representative to NATO (2017–2021).

124.name: Connie Mack
124.alma_mater: University of Florida; University of Miami
124.biography: Connie Mack III (Cornelius A. McGillicuddy III) served in the U.S. House from Florida (1983–1989) and in the U.S. Senate from Florida (1989–2001). A Republican, he became chairman of the Senate Republican Conference (1997–2001) and was influential on fiscal and national security issues. He was considered as a vice-presidential prospect in 1996 and 2000.

125.name: William Cohen
125.alma_mater: Bowdoin College; Boston University School of Law
125.biography: William S. Cohen is a Maine politician who served as a U.S. Representative (1973–1979) and U.S. Senator (1979–1997) as a Republican. Known as a moderate, he later served as U.S. Secretary of Defense (1997–2001) under President Bill Clinton. After leaving office, he worked in law, consulting, and public policy and authored books.

126.name: J. Kerrey
126.biography: Joseph Robert “Bob” Kerrey (born 1943) served as Governor of Nebraska (1983–1987) and U.S. Senator from Nebraska (1989–2001). A former U.S. Navy SEAL, he received the Medal of Honor for actions in Vietnam. In the Senate he was a prominent Democrat and ran for the Democratic presidential nomination in 1992. After leaving office, he led The New School (2001–2010).

127.name: John Glenn
127.alma_mater: Muskingum University; University of Maryland, College Park
127.biography: John Glenn (1921–2016) was a U.S. Marine Corps aviator and NASA astronaut, the first American to orbit Earth (1962). After leaving NASA, he was elected as a Democrat to the U.S. Senate from Ohio, serving from 1974 to 1999. He ran for the Democratic presidential nomination in 1984 and in 1998 returned to space aboard Space Shuttle Discovery at age 77.

128.name: Richard Bryan
128.alma_mater: University of Nevada, Reno; University of California, Berkeley
128.biography: Richard H. Bryan (born 1937) is a Nevada attorney and retired politician. He served in the Nevada State Senate, as Nevada attorney general, and as the 25th governor of Nevada (1983–1989). Bryan was then elected to the U.S. Senate, representing Nevada from 1989 to 2001 as a Democrat.

129.name: Bob Graham
129.biography: Bob Graham (1936–2024) was an American politician who served as governor of Florida (1979–1987) and as a U.S. senator from Florida (1987–2005). A Democrat, he previously served in the Florida House and Senate. In the U.S. Senate he was known for work on intelligence and national security and chaired the Senate Select Committee on Intelligence (2001–2003).

130.name: John Kerry
130.alma_mater: Yale University; Boston College Law School
130.biography: John Forbes Kerry (born 1943) served as a U.S. Senator from Massachusetts (1985–2013) after serving as the state’s lieutenant governor. A Vietnam War naval veteran, he became the Democratic nominee for president in 2004. Kerry later served as U.S. Secretary of State (2013–2017) and as U.S. Special Presidential Envoy for Climate (2021–2024).

131.name: Max Baucus
131.alma_mater: Stanford University; University of Virginia School of Law
131.biography: Max Baucus (born 1941) served as a U.S. Representative from Montana (1975–1978) and U.S. Senator from Montana (1978–2014). A Democrat, he chaired the Senate Finance Committee and influenced major tax and health policy debates, including the Affordable Care Act era. After leaving the Senate, he was U.S. Ambassador to China (2014–2017).

132.name: J. Johnston
132.biography: John W. Johnston (1818–1889) was a Virginia lawyer and politician from Abingdon. After serving in the Virginia State Senate, he represented Virginia in the U.S. Senate following the state’s readmission after the Civil War, serving from 1870 to 1883. He was initially barred under the Fourteenth Amendment but was seated after a successful petition. He was the first former Confederate to serve in the U.S. Senate.

133.name: Charles Robb
133.alma_mater: University of Wisconsin–Madison; University of Virginia
133.biography: Charles “Chuck” Robb (born 1939) is a former U.S. Marine officer and politician. He served as Governor of Virginia (1982–1986) and as U.S. Senator from Virginia (1989–2001), a Democrat. He is married to Lynda Bird Johnson, making him Lyndon B. Johnson’s son-in-law. He lost his 2000 Senate reelection bid to Republican George Allen.

134.name: Pat Roberts
134.biography: Pat Roberts (born 1936) is a retired Republican politician and former journalist who served as U.S. Senator from Kansas (1997–2021) after serving in the U.S. House representing Kansas (1981–1997). A Kansas State University graduate and former U.S. Marine Corps officer, he chaired the House Agriculture Committee and served on the Senate Intelligence Committee, helping lead a review of pre-Iraq War intelligence.

135.name: Mary Landrieu
135.biography: Mary Landrieu (born 1955) is a Louisiana Democrat who served in the U.S. Senate from 1997 to 2015. Previously she was a member of the Louisiana House of Representatives (1980–1988) and Louisiana State Treasurer (1988–1996). In the Senate she chaired the Small Business and Entrepreneurship Committee (2009–2014) and the Energy and Natural Resources Committee (2014–2015). She gained national attention after Hurricane Katrina.

136.name: Tim Johnson
136.biography: Tim Johnson (1946–2024) was a South Dakota Democrat who served in the state legislature (1979–1987), the U.S. House representing South Dakota’s at-large district (1987–1997), and the U.S. Senate (1997–2015). A lawyer by training, he became a senior Senate Democrat and was known as a pragmatic voice in a heavily Republican-leaning state.

137.name: Max Cleland
137.biography: Max Cleland (1942–2021) was a Georgia Democrat and decorated Vietnam War veteran who lost three limbs in combat. He served in the Georgia State Senate (1971–1975), was Administrator of Veterans Affairs under President Carter (1977–1981), and Georgia Secretary of State (1982–1996). Elected to the U.S. Senate, he represented Georgia from 1997 to 2003. Later he served on the Export-Import Bank board and led the American Battle Monuments Commission.

138.name: Wayne Allard
138.biography: Alan Wayne Allard (born 1943) is a veterinarian and Republican politician from Colorado. He served in the Colorado State Senate (1983–1991), the U.S. House of Representatives (1991–1997), and the U.S. Senate (1997–2009). He did not seek re-election in 2008 and left office in 2009, later working as a lobbyist in Washington, D.C.

139.name: Michael Enzi
139.alma_mater: George Washington University; University of Denver
139.biography: Michael "Mike" Enzi (1944–2021) was a Republican U.S. Senator from Wyoming (1997–2021). An Air National Guard veteran, he previously served as mayor of Gillette and in the Wyoming House and Senate. In the U.S. Senate he became known for work on budget, health, and labor issues and was among Wyoming’s longest-serving senators.

140.name: Chuck Hagel
140.biography: Chuck Hagel (born 1946) is a Republican politician and Vietnam War Army veteran. He served as U.S. senator from Nebraska from 1997 to 2009, after being elected in 1996 and reelected in 2002. After leaving the Senate, he chaired the President’s Intelligence Advisory Board (2009–2013) and served as U.S. secretary of defense (2013–2015) under President Barack Obama.

141.name: Jack Reed
141.alma_mater: United States Military Academy; Harvard University; Harvard Law School
141.biography: John F. "Jack" Reed (born 1949) is a Democratic politician and former U.S. Army officer serving as U.S. senator from Rhode Island, elected in 1996. He previously represented Rhode Island’s 2nd congressional district (1991–1997). A West Point graduate, he served on active duty in the Army from 1971 to 1979 and later earned degrees from Harvard and Harvard Law School.

142.name: Gordon Smith
142.biography: Gordon Harold Smith (born 1952) is an Oregon politician and businessman who served as a Republican U.S. Senator from Oregon from 1997 to 2009, after being elected in 1996 and reelected in 2002. After leaving the Senate, he led the National Association of Broadcasters (2009–2022). He has also held leadership roles in the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints.

143.name: Richard Durbin
143.alma_mater: Georgetown University (School of Foreign Service); Georgetown University Law Center
143.biography: Richard J. Durbin (born 1944) is a Democratic politician from Illinois. He served in the U.S. House of Representatives (1983–1997) before becoming U.S. senator (1997–present). A longtime member of Senate Democratic leadership, he has served as Democratic whip since 2005 and chaired the Senate Judiciary Committee (2021–2025). Before elective office, he worked as counsel in Illinois state government and practiced law.

144.name: Samuel Brownback
144.alma_mater: Kansas State University; University of Kansas
144.biography: Sam Brownback (born 1956) is a Republican politician and attorney. He served in the U.S. House representing Kansas’s 2nd district (1995–1996) and as U.S. Senator from Kansas (1996–2011). He was Governor of Kansas (2011–2018) and later served as U.S. Ambassador-at-Large for International Religious Freedom (2018–2021). He ran for the Republican presidential nomination in 2008.

145.name: Tim Hutchinson
145.alma_mater: Bob Jones University; Ouachita Baptist University
145.biography: Tim Hutchinson (born 1949) is an Arkansas Republican politician and lobbyist. He served in the U.S. House of Representatives from Arkansas (1991–1997), then as U.S. Senator from Arkansas (1997–2003), becoming the first Republican elected to the state’s U.S. Senate seat since Reconstruction. He later worked as a Washington, D.C., lobbyist.

146.name: Jeff Sessions
146.biography: Jeff Sessions (born 1946) is an Alabama Republican politician and attorney. He served as U.S. attorney for the Southern District of Alabama (1981–1993), Alabama attorney general (1995–1997), and U.S. senator from Alabama (1997–2017). He resigned from the Senate to become U.S. attorney general (2017–2018) under President Donald Trump.

147.name: Susan Collins
147.biography: Susan Margaret Collins (born 1952) is a Republican U.S. senator from Maine, serving since 1997. Raised in Caribou, Maine, she graduated from St. Lawrence University. She began her career as a staff assistant to Sen. William Cohen, later becoming staff director for a Senate Governmental Affairs subcommittee. She served in Maine state government, including as commissioner of Professional and Financial Regulation, before winning election to the Senate.

148.name: Robert Torricelli
148.biography: Robert G. Torricelli (born 1951) is a New Jersey Democrat who served in the U.S. House (NJ-9, 1983–1997) and U.S. Senate (1997–2003). He chaired the Democratic Senatorial Campaign Committee and was active on foreign policy issues. In 2002 he ended his reelection bid after a Senate ethics admonishment tied to campaign finance matters. After Congress he worked in consulting and real estate.

149.name: Peter Fitzgerald
149.biography: Peter G. Fitzgerald (born 1960) is a retired lawyer and Republican politician. He served in the Illinois State Senate (1993–1998) and then as U.S. Senator from Illinois (1999–2005), after defeating incumbent Carol Moseley Braun in 1998. Known for an independent streak, he did not seek reelection and was succeeded by Barack Obama. After leaving politics, he helped found Chain Bridge Bank.

150.name: Blanche Lincoln
150.biography: Blanche Lambert Lincoln (born 1960) is an Arkansas Democrat who served in the U.S. House for Arkansas’s 1st district (1993–1997) and in the U.S. Senate (1999–2011). Elected to the Senate in 1998, she became the first woman elected U.S. senator from Arkansas since 1932. She chaired the Senate Agriculture, Nutrition and Forestry Committee and lost reelection in 2010 to John Boozman.

151.name: George Voinovich
151.biography: George V. Voinovich (1936–2016) was a Republican politician who served as U.S. senator from Ohio (1999–2011). He previously was mayor of Cleveland (1980–1989) and governor of Ohio (1991–1998). Earlier he served as Ohio lieutenant governor and held state and local posts. Known for fiscal and managerial focus, he was active on Senate committees on government affairs, budget, and energy and environmental issues.

152.name: James Bunning
152.biography: Jim Bunning (1931–2017) was a Kentucky Republican who served in the U.S. House (1987–1999) and U.S. Senate (1999–2011). Before politics he was a Major League Baseball pitcher (1955–1971), threw a perfect game in 1964, and was elected to the National Baseball Hall of Fame. He was known for fiscal conservatism and outspoken positions.

153.name: Michael Crapo
153.alma_mater: Brigham Young University; Harvard Law School
153.biography: Michael Dean "Mike" Crapo (born 1951) is a Republican U.S. senator from Idaho, serving since 1999. He previously represented Idaho’s 2nd congressional district in the U.S. House (1993–1999) and served in the Idaho Senate (1984–1992), including as president pro tempore. An Idaho Falls native, he earned degrees from Brigham Young University and Harvard Law School and practiced law before entering politics.

154.name: Charles Schumer
154.biography: Charles E. "Chuck" Schumer (born 1950) is a Democrat serving as U.S. Senator from New York since 1999. He previously served in the New York State Assembly (1975–1980) and the U.S. House (1981–1999). He became New York’s senior senator in 2001. Schumer has led Senate Democrats since 2017, serving as Senate Majority Leader (2021–2025) and Senate Minority Leader (2017–2021; since 2025).

155.name: Evan Bayh
155.biography: Birch Evans “Evan” Bayh III (born 1955) is an Indiana Democrat who served as U.S. senator from Indiana (1999–2011) and previously as the 46th governor of Indiana (1989–1997). He also served as Indiana secretary of state (1986–1988). Son of former senator Birch Bayh, he was elected to the Senate in 1998 and reelected in 2004, retiring in 2010.

156.name: John Edwards
156.alma_mater: North Carolina State University; University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
156.biography: John Edwards (born 1953) is a Democratic former U.S. Senator from North Carolina (1999–2005). A trial lawyer, he defeated Republican incumbent Lauch Faircloth in 1998. Edwards ran for the Democratic presidential nomination in 2004 and 2008 and was the 2004 Democratic vice-presidential nominee with John Kerry. He declined to seek Senate re-election in 2004.

157.name: Lincoln Chafee
157.biography: Lincoln Chafee served as U.S. Senator from Rhode Island (1999–2007), appointed after his father John Chafee’s death and elected to a full term in 2000. Previously he was mayor of Warwick, Rhode Island (1993–1999). A moderate Republican in the Senate, he later became an independent and then a Democrat. He served as governor of Rhode Island (2011–2015).

158.name: Zell Miller
158.biography: Zell Bryan Miller (1932–2018) was a Georgia politician who served as lieutenant governor (1975–1991), governor (1991–1999), and U.S. senator from Georgia (2000–2005), appointed and later elected to fill a vacancy. A conservative Democrat, he became nationally prominent for his support of Republican George W. Bush in 2004 and spoke at both the 1992 Democratic and 2004 Republican conventions.

159.name: Deborah Stabenow
159.biography: Deborah “Debbie” Stabenow (born 1950) served as a Democratic U.S. senator from Michigan from 2001 to 2025, the state’s first female U.S. senator. Previously she represented Michigan’s 8th district in the U.S. House (1997–2001) and served in Michigan state and local government. She chaired the Senate Agriculture Committee (2011–2015, 2021–2025) and led the Senate Democratic Policy Committee starting in 2017.

160.name: Earl Nelson
160.biography: Earl Benjamin "Ben" Nelson (born 1941) is a Nebraska Democrat who served as U.S. senator (2001–2013). He previously was Nebraska’s governor (1991–1999) after a career as an insurance executive. Nelson lost a 1996 Senate race to Chuck Hagel, then won Nebraska’s other Senate seat in 2000 and was reelected in 2006. He is known as a centrist Democrat.

161.name: Mark Dayton
161.biography: Mark Dayton is a Minnesota Democratic–Farmer–Labor politician who served as U.S. Senator from Minnesota (2001–2007). Earlier he was Minnesota State Auditor (1991–1995) and worked as a legislative aide to Sen. Walter Mondale and Gov. Rudy Perpich. After leaving the Senate, he was elected governor of Minnesota, serving from 2011 to 2019.

162.name: Clarence Nelson
162.alma_mater: Yale University; University of Virginia School of Law
162.biography: Clarence William "Bill" Nelson (born 1942) is an American politician who served as a Democratic U.S. Senator from Florida (2001–2019). Earlier, he flew in space aboard Space Shuttle Columbia in 1986 while in Congress, served in the U.S. House representing Florida (1979–1991), and as Florida treasurer/insurance commissioner (1995–2001). He later became NASA administrator (2021–2025).

163.name: Jean Carnahan
163.biography: Jean Carnahan was a Missouri Democrat who became the state’s first woman U.S. senator. After her husband, Gov. Mel Carnahan, was posthumously elected in 2000, she was appointed to fill the Senate seat and served as Missouri’s junior senator from 2001 to 2002. She previously served as Missouri’s first lady (1993–2000) and was also a writer.

164.name: Thomas Carper
164.alma_mater: Ohio State University; University of Delaware
164.biography: Thomas Richard "Tom" Carper (born 1947) is a Democrat who served as U.S. Senator from Delaware (2001–2025). He previously represented Delaware at-large in the U.S. House (1983–1993), was Delaware state treasurer (1977–1983), and served as governor of Delaware (1993–2001). A former U.S. Navy flight officer, he later earned an MBA from the University of Delaware.

165.name: Jon Corzine
165.biography: Jon Corzine (born 1947) is a Democrat who served as U.S. Senator from New Jersey (2001–2006), resigning after being elected the 54th governor of New Jersey (2006–2010). Previously, he built a career at Goldman Sachs, becoming its CEO and later chairman, and entered politics after self-funding his 2000 Senate campaign. After leaving office, he led MF Global until its 2011 collapse.

166.name: John Ensign
166.biography: John Eric Ensign (born 1958) is a Republican former U.S. Senator from Nevada (2001–2011). A veterinarian, he previously served as U.S. Representative for Nevada’s 1st district (1995–1999). Ensign resigned from the Senate in 2011 amid a Senate Ethics Committee investigation related to an extramarital affair and efforts to conceal it, then returned to Nevada and resumed veterinary work.

167.name: Hillary Clinton
167.biography: Hillary Rodham Clinton (born 1947) is a Democratic politician, lawyer, and diplomat. She served as U.S. Senator from New York (2001–2009), after being First Lady of the United States (1993–2001). She was U.S. Secretary of State (2009–2013) under Barack Obama and the Democratic nominee for president in 2016, winning the popular vote but losing the Electoral College.

168.name: George Allen
168.biography: George Felix Allen (born 1952) is a Republican politician and lawyer from Virginia. He served in the Virginia House of Delegates (1983–1991), briefly represented Virginia’s 7th district in the U.S. House (1991–1993), and was governor of Virginia (1994–1998). He was elected U.S. senator from Virginia (2001–2007), defeating incumbent Chuck Robb, and later returned to private-sector work after leaving office.

169.name: Maria Cantwell
169.biography: Maria Cantwell (born 1958) is a Democrat serving as U.S. senator from Washington since 2001. She previously served in the Washington House of Representatives (1987–1993) and one term in the U.S. House representing Washington (1993–1995), losing reelection in 1994. After working at RealNetworks, she returned to politics and defeated incumbent Sen. Slade Gorton in 2000.

170.name: James Jeffords
170.biography: James M. Jeffords was a Vermont lawyer and politician who served in the Vermont Senate and as Vermont attorney general. He represented Vermont at-large in the U.S. House (1975–1989) and served as U.S. senator (1989–2007). Elected as a Republican, he left the party in 2001 to become an independent and caucused with Senate Democrats, shifting control of the chamber.

171.name: Saxby Chambliss
171.biography: Clarence Saxby Chambliss (born 1943) is an American lawyer and Republican politician. He served as U.S. Representative from Georgia (1995–2003) and U.S. Senator from Georgia (2003–2015), first elected in 2002 by defeating Max Cleland. In the Senate he chaired the Senate Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition and Forestry (2005–2007) and later served as ranking Republican on the Senate Select Committee on Intelligence.

172.name: Norm Coleman
172.biography: Norman Bertram Coleman Jr. (born 1949) is an attorney and politician who served as mayor of Saint Paul, Minnesota (1994–2002) and as U.S. Senator from Minnesota (2003–2009). Elected mayor as a Democrat/DFL, he switched to the Republican Party in 1996. He won the 2002 Senate election and lost reelection after a close 2008 contest.

173.name: James Talent
173.biography: James Matthew Talent is a Republican politician from Missouri. He served in the U.S. House of Representatives (1993–2001) and then as U.S. Senator (2002–2007), winning a 2002 special election and losing reelection in 2006. Earlier he was a state legislator and chaired the Missouri Republican Party. After the Senate he worked in policy and defense-related roles and as a political commentator.

174.name: Elizabeth Dole
174.biography: Elizabeth Dole is a Republican politician and attorney who served as U.S. Senator from North Carolina (2003–2009). She held senior federal posts in five administrations, including U.S. Secretary of Transportation (1983–1987) and U.S. Secretary of Labor (1989–1990). She was president of the American Red Cross (1991–1999) and briefly sought the 2000 Republican presidential nomination.

175.name: Lindsey Graham
175.biography: Lindsey Olin Graham (born 1955) is a Republican U.S. senator from South Carolina, serving since 2003. He previously served in the South Carolina House (1993–1995) and the U.S. House representing South Carolina’s 3rd district (1995–2003). A lawyer and Air Force JAG veteran, he later served in the Air Force Reserve and chaired the Senate Judiciary Committee (2019–2021).

176.name: John Cornyn
176.alma_mater: Trinity University; St. Mary's University School of Law; University of Virginia School of Law
176.biography: John Cornyn III (born 1952) is a Republican U.S. Senator from Texas, first elected in 2002. He previously served as a judge on Texas’s 37th District Court (1985–1991), an associate justice of the Texas Supreme Court (1991–1997), and Attorney General of Texas (1999–2002). In the Senate he chaired the National Republican Senatorial Committee (2009–2013) and was Senate Republican whip (2013–2019).

177.name: Mark Pryor
177.biography: Mark Lunsford Pryor (born 1963) is an Arkansas attorney and Democratic politician who served as U.S. senator from Arkansas (2003–2015). Son of former senator David Pryor, he previously served in the Arkansas House of Representatives (1991–1995) and as Arkansas Attorney General (1999–2003). He was elected to the Senate in 2002 and lost reelection in 2014.

178.name: John Sununu
178.alma_mater: Massachusetts Institute of Technology; Harvard University; Tufts University
178.biography: John E. Sununu (born 1964) is a New Hampshire Republican who served in the U.S. House (1997–2003) and U.S. Senate (2003–2009). Trained as an engineer, he won election to the Senate after six House terms and became one of its youngest members. He was defeated for reelection in 2008 by Democrat Jeanne Shaheen.

179.name: Lamar Alexander
179.alma_mater: Vanderbilt University; New York University School of Law
179.biography: Lamar Alexander (born 1940) is a Republican politician and attorney who served as U.S. senator from Tennessee (2003–2021). He previously was governor of Tennessee (1979–1987), chaired the National Governors Association (1985–1986), and served as U.S. secretary of education under President George H. W. Bush (1991–1993), backing the America 2000 education reform initiative.

180.name: Lisa Murkowski
180.alma_mater: Georgetown University, Willamette University College of Law
180.biography: Lisa Murkowski (born 1957) is a Republican U.S. senator from Alaska, appointed in 2002 to fill the seat vacated by her father, Frank Murkowski, and later elected to full terms. She previously served in the Alaska House of Representatives (1999–2002). Known as a more moderate Republican, she has often played a swing role in closely divided Senate votes.

181.name: Kenneth Salazar
181.biography: Kenneth Lee "Ken" Salazar is a Democratic politician and lawyer who served as U.S. Senator from Colorado (2005–2009), becoming the state’s first Latino senator. Previously he was Attorney General of Colorado (1999–2005). He resigned from the Senate to become U.S. Secretary of the Interior under President Barack Obama (2009–2013) and later served as U.S. Ambassador to Mexico (2021–2025).

182.name: John Thune
182.alma_mater: Biola University; University of South Dakota
182.biography: John Randolph Thune (born 1961) is a Republican U.S. senator from South Dakota, serving since 2005. He previously represented South Dakota at-large in the U.S. House (1997–2003). After narrowly losing a 2002 Senate race to Tim Johnson, he defeated Senate Democratic leader Tom Daschle in 2004. Thune has held multiple Senate GOP leadership roles, including majority leader beginning in 2025.

183.name: Melquiades Martinez
183.biography: Melquíades Martínez (born 1946) is a Cuban-American Republican who served as U.S. Senator from Florida (2005–2009), the first Cuban-American in the Senate. He previously led Florida’s Department of Community Affairs and was U.S. Secretary of Housing and Urban Development (2001–2004) under President George W. Bush. He was also general chairman of the Republican National Committee (2006–2007) and resigned from the Senate in 2009.

184.name: Robert Menendez
184.alma_mater: Saint Peter's University; Rutgers Law School
