184.biography: Robert “Bob” Menendez (born 1954) is a New Jersey Democrat who served in the U.S. House (1993–2006) and U.S. Senate (2006–2024). He began in local politics in Union City, including the school board and as mayor, and served in the New Jersey General Assembly and Senate. He resigned from the U.S. Senate in 2024 after a federal corruption conviction.

185.name: James DeMint
185.biography: James (Jim) DeMint (born 1951) is a Republican who served as U.S. Representative for South Carolina’s 4th district (1999–2005) and U.S. Senator from South Carolina (2005–2013). A prominent conservative and Tea Party ally, he was reelected to the Senate in 2010 but resigned in 2013 to lead The Heritage Foundation. He founded the Senate Conservatives Fund and later worked with other conservative organizations.

186.name: David Vitter
186.alma_mater: Harvard University; Magdalen College, Oxford; Tulane University
186.biography: David Vitter (born 1961) is a Republican politician from Louisiana. He served in the Louisiana House of Representatives (1992–1999), the U.S. House (1999–2005), and the U.S. Senate (2005–2017). He was the first Republican popularly elected to the U.S. Senate from Louisiana. He ran for governor in 2015 and lost to John Bel Edwards.

187.name: Richard Burr
187.biography: Richard Mauze Burr (born 1955) is a Republican politician who served as U.S. senator from North Carolina (2005–2023). He previously represented North Carolina’s 5th congressional district in the U.S. House (1995–2005), first elected in 1994. A Wake Forest University graduate and former lawn-equipment sales manager, Burr chaired the Senate Intelligence Committee from 2015 to 2020 and did not seek reelection in 2022.

188.name: Johnny Isakson
188.biography: Johnny Isakson (1944–2021) was a Republican U.S. senator from Georgia (2005–2019), resigning due to health concerns. A real estate executive and former Georgia Air National Guard member, he served in the Georgia House, including as minority leader, and in the Georgia Senate. He also served in the U.S. House of Representatives (1999–2005) before winning a Senate seat.

189.name: Barack Obama
189.alma_mater: Occidental College; Columbia University; Harvard University
189.biography: Barack Obama served as a U.S. Senator from Illinois (2005–2008) after serving in the Illinois Senate (1997–2004). He gained national prominence with his 2004 Democratic National Convention keynote address, then won the 2004 U.S. Senate election. Obama ran for president in 2008, became the 44th president of the United States (2009–2017), and was re-elected in 2012.

190.name: Thomas Coburn
190.biography: Tom Coburn (1948–2020) was a Republican physician and politician from Oklahoma. An obstetrician in Muskogee, he served in the U.S. House (1995–2001) and the U.S. Senate (2005–2015). Elected to the Senate in 2004 and re-elected in 2010, he became known as a fiscal and social conservative. He resigned in 2015 after a recurrence of prostate cancer.

191.name: Sherrod Brown
191.biography: Sherrod Brown (born 1952) is an Ohio Democrat who served as U.S. senator from 2007 to 2025. Earlier, he was Ohio secretary of state (1983–1991), a member of the Ohio House, and U.S. representative for Ohio’s 13th district (1993–2007). Elected to the Senate in 2006, he defeated Mike DeWine and won reelection in 2012 and 2018.

192.name: Robert Casey
192.alma_mater: College of the Holy Cross; George Washington University Law School
192.biography: Robert P. Casey (1932–2000) was a Pennsylvania Democrat who served as Auditor General (1969–1977), Treasurer (1977–1981), and Governor (1987–1995). A labor-aligned, socially conservative Democrat, he pursued economic development and education initiatives and was prominent nationally for opposition to abortion rights. He previously worked as an attorney and held various state public offices.

193.name: Amy Klobuchar
193.alma_mater: Yale University; University of Chicago Law School
193.biography: Amy Klobuchar (born 1960) is a Minnesota Democrat (DFL) serving as U.S. senator from Minnesota since 2007. Previously, she was Hennepin County attorney (1999–2007) and worked in private practice. First elected to the Senate in 2006, she became Minnesota’s senior senator in 2009. She ran for the 2020 Democratic presidential nomination and has focused on issues including antitrust, infrastructure, and election security.

194.name: Jim Webb
194.alma_mater: United States Naval Academy; Georgetown University; Harvard University
194.biography: Jim Webb is a retired U.S. Marine Corps officer, author, and politician. He served as U.S. Senator from Virginia (2007–2013), after switching from the Republican Party to the Democratic Party in 2006. Webb previously held executive-branch posts including Secretary of the Navy (1987–1988) and Assistant Secretary of Defense for Reserve Affairs (1984–1987). He later explored a 2016 presidential run.

195.name: Claire McCaskill
195.alma_mater: University of Missouri; University of Missouri School of Law
195.biography: Claire McCaskill (born 1953) is a Democratic politician and attorney who served as U.S. Senator from Missouri (2007–2019), the first woman elected to the Senate from Missouri. She previously served in the Missouri House (1983–1989), as Jackson County Prosecutor (1993–1998), and as Missouri State Auditor (1999–2007). She ran for governor in 2004, losing to Matt Blunt.

196.name: Bob Corker
196.biography: Robert P. “Bob” Corker Jr. (born 1952) is a Republican businessman and politician who served as U.S. Senator from Tennessee (2007–2019). He previously was mayor of Chattanooga (2001–2005) and Tennessee Commissioner of Finance and Administration (1995–1996). In the Senate he chaired the Foreign Relations Committee (2015–2019) and was known for work on foreign policy and fiscal issues.

197.name: Roger Wicker
197.alma_mater: University of Mississippi; University of Mississippi School of Law
197.biography: Roger F. Wicker (born 1951) is a Republican U.S. senator from Mississippi, serving since 2007 after appointment and subsequent elections. He previously served in the Mississippi State Senate (1988–1995) and represented Mississippi’s 1st congressional district in the U.S. House (1995–2007). An attorney, he was a U.S. Air Force officer (1976–1980) and a reservist until 2003.

198.name: Jon Tester
198.biography: Jon Tester is a Montana farmer and Democrat who served as U.S. senator from Montana from 2007 to 2025. He previously served in the Montana Senate (1999–2007), including as Senate president (2005–2007). First elected to the U.S. Senate in 2006, he won reelection in 2012 and 2018 and lost his 2024 bid to Republican Tim Sheehy.

199.name: Benjamin Cardin
199.alma_mater: University of Maryland, College Park; University of Maryland School of Law
199.biography: Benjamin L. Cardin (born 1943) is a Maryland Democrat who served in the Maryland House of Delegates (1967–1987), including as speaker (1979–1987). He was U.S. representative for Maryland’s 3rd district (1987–2007) and U.S. senator from Maryland (2007–2025), succeeding Paul Sarbanes. He became Maryland’s senior senator in 2017 and won reelection in 2012 and 2018.

200.name: Bernard Sanders
200.party: Independent (served as Senate Democrat caucus ally)
200.biography: Bernard "Bernie" Sanders is an independent U.S. Senator from Vermont, caucusing with Democrats. He previously served as U.S. Representative for Vermont’s at-large district (1991–2007) and as mayor of Burlington (1981–1989). A prominent progressive, he chaired the Senate Veterans’ Affairs Committee and later the Senate Budget Committee. Sanders ran for the Democratic presidential nomination in 2016 and 2020.

201.name: Sheldon Whitehouse
201.alma_mater: Yale University; University of Virginia School of Law
201.biography: Sheldon Whitehouse (born 1955) is a Democratic U.S. senator from Rhode Island, serving since 2007 after defeating Lincoln Chafee. An attorney, he was U.S. Attorney for the District of Rhode Island (1993–1998) and Rhode Island attorney general (1999–2003). Known as a progressive and prominent climate advocate, he became chair of the Senate Budget Committee in 2023.

202.name: John Barrasso
202.biography: John Barrasso is an American physician and Republican politician serving as U.S. Senator from Wyoming since 2007. He previously served in the Wyoming State Senate (2003–2007). After the death of Senator Craig L. Thomas, Barrasso was appointed to the U.S. Senate in 2007, then elected to the seat in 2008 and reelected thereafter. He has held Senate Republican leadership and committee roles.

203.name: Michael Bennet
203.biography: Michael Farrand Bennet (born 1964) is a Democratic U.S. senator from Colorado, appointed in 2009 to replace Ken Salazar and later elected to full terms. Before the Senate he worked at the Anschutz Investment Company, served as chief of staff to Denver mayor John Hickenlooper, and was superintendent of Denver Public Schools. Earlier he worked in the Clinton Justice Department and for Ohio governor Richard Celeste.

204.name: Kirsten Gillibrand
204.alma_mater: Dartmouth College; UCLA School of Law
204.biography: Kirsten Gillibrand (born 1966) is a Democratic U.S. senator from New York, serving since 2009 after being appointed to succeed Hillary Clinton. She previously represented New York’s 20th congressional district in the U.S. House (2007–2009). Trained as a lawyer, she worked in government and private practice and on Clinton’s 2000 Senate campaign before winning election to Congress.

205.name: Mike Johanns
205.alma_mater: Saint Mary's University of Minnesota; Creighton University School of Law
205.biography: Michael O. Johanns (born 1950) is a Republican politician and attorney who served as U.S. senator from Nebraska (2009–2015). He was mayor of Lincoln (1991–1998), governor of Nebraska (1999–2005), and U.S. secretary of agriculture under President George W. Bush (2005–2007). Earlier he served on the Lancaster County Board and the Lincoln City Council.

206.name: Mark Udall
206.alma_mater: Williams College; University of Colorado Boulder
206.biography: Mark Udall is a Democratic politician who served as U.S. senator from Colorado (2009–2015). He previously represented Colorado’s 2nd congressional district in the U.S. House (1999–2009) and served in the Colorado House of Representatives. A member of the Udall political family, he emphasized renewable energy, conservation, and public lands. He lost reelection in 2014 to Republican Cory Gardner.

207.name: Kay Hagan
207.alma_mater: Florida State University, Wake Forest University School of Law
207.biography: Kay Hagan (1953–2019) was a North Carolina Democrat who served as U.S. senator from 2009 to 2015. Previously, she represented Guilford County in the North Carolina Senate (1999–2009). In 2008 she defeated incumbent Sen. Elizabeth Dole, and in 2014 she lost reelection to Thom Tillis. Before elective office she worked as an attorney and banking executive.

208.name: Mark Begich
208.biography: Mark Begich is an American politician who served as a Democratic U.S. Senator from Alaska (2009–2015). He previously was mayor of Anchorage (2003–2009) and served on the Anchorage Assembly (1995–2003). Begich narrowly defeated incumbent Republican Ted Stevens in 2008 but lost reelection to Dan Sullivan in 2014. After leaving the Senate, he remained active in Alaska politics and public affairs.

209.name: Roland Burris
209.alma_mater: University of Illinois at Urbana–Champaign; Southern Illinois University School of Law
209.biography: Roland Wallace Burris (born 1937) is an Illinois attorney and Democratic politician. He served as Illinois Comptroller (1979–1991) and Illinois Attorney General (1991–1995). In 2009 he was appointed to the U.S. Senate from Illinois, serving until 2010. Burris made multiple unsuccessful bids for governor and ran unsuccessfully for Chicago mayor in 1995.

210.name: Paul Kirk
210.biography: Paul G. Kirk Jr. is a Massachusetts Democrat who served as interim U.S. senator in 2009–2010, appointed after Ted Kennedy’s death. A longtime party leader, he was chair of the Democratic National Committee (1985–1989) and played prominent roles in national Democratic politics. He also served as a U.S. Navy Reserve officer and worked as a lawyer and political strategist.

211.name: George LeMieux
211.alma_mater: Florida State University; University of Miami
211.biography: George LeMieux (born 1969) is a Republican former U.S. Senator from Florida, appointed in 2009 by Gov. Charlie Crist to fill the vacancy after Mel Martínez resigned; he served until 2011. He previously worked as Crist’s chief of staff and as Deputy Florida Attorney General. After leaving the Senate, he became a lawyer and served as chairman of the Florida-based law firm Gunster.

212.name: Mark Warner
212.biography: Mark Robert Warner (born 1954) is a Democratic politician and businessman serving as U.S. senator from Virginia since 2009. He previously served as Virginia’s 69th governor (2002–2006). Warner was elected to the Senate in 2008 and reelected in 2014 and 2020, becoming Virginia’s senior senator in 2013. He has served in Senate Democratic leadership and as vice chair of the Senate Intelligence Committee.

213.name: Jeff Merkley
213.biography: Jeff Merkley (born 1956) is a Democratic U.S. Senator from Oregon, first elected in 2008 after defeating incumbent Gordon Smith. He previously served in the Oregon House of Representatives (1999–2009) and was Speaker (2007–2009). In the Senate he is known as a progressive and was the only senator to endorse Bernie Sanders in the 2016 Democratic presidential primaries.

214.name: Tom Udall
214.alma_mater: University of New Mexico; University of New Mexico School of Law
214.biography: Thomas Stewart Udall (born 1948) is a Democrat who served as U.S. senator from New Mexico (2009–2021). He previously was New Mexico attorney general (1991–1999) and U.S. representative for New Mexico’s 3rd district (1999–2009). A member of the Udall political family, he did not seek reelection in 2020 and later served as U.S. ambassador to New Zealand and Samoa.

215.name: Jeanne Shaheen
215.alma_mater: Shippensburg University of Pennsylvania; University of Mississippi
215.biography: Jeanne Shaheen (born 1947) is a Democratic U.S. Senator from New Hampshire, serving since 2009. Previously, she served in the New Hampshire Senate and was governor of New Hampshire from 1997 to 2003, the state’s first elected female governor. She lost a 2002 U.S. Senate race to John E. Sununu but defeated him in a 2008 rematch.

216.name: James Risch
216.biography: James E. "Jim" Risch (born 1943) is a Republican U.S. senator from Idaho, serving since 2009. An attorney, he previously served as Ada County prosecuting attorney, a longtime member of the Idaho Senate, and lieutenant governor under Dirk Kempthorne and Butch Otter. He briefly served as governor in 2006 between administrations.

217.name: Ted Kaufman
217.biography: Edward “Ted” Kaufman (born 1939) is a Delaware Democrat who served as a U.S. senator from 2009–2010, appointed to fill Joe Biden’s seat after Biden became vice president. A longtime Biden adviser and former chief of staff, he later helped lead Biden’s 2008 transition. After leaving the Senate, Kaufman chaired the Congressional Oversight Panel overseeing the Troubled Asset Relief Program.

218.name: Al Franken
218.biography: Alan “Al” Franken (born 1951) is an American comedian and writer who became a Democratic U.S. Senator from Minnesota (2009–2018). He rose to prominence as a writer and performer on Saturday Night Live and later hosted political radio and wrote bestselling political books. Elected in 2008 after a recount, he served on major committees including Judiciary and Health, Education, Labor and Pensions before resigning in 2018.

219.name: John Boozman
219.alma_mater: University of Arkansas; Southern College of Optometry
219.biography: John Nichols Boozman (born 1950) is a Republican U.S. senator from Arkansas, in office since 2011. He previously served in the U.S. House representing Arkansas’s 3rd district (2001–2011) after winning a 2001 special election. An optometrist by profession, he co-founded a private practice in 1977 and did volunteer work for low-income families.

220.name: John Hoeven
220.biography: John Henry Hoeven III (born 1957) is a Republican U.S. senator from North Dakota, serving since 2011. He previously served as North Dakota’s governor (2000–2010) and worked in banking, including as president of the state-owned Bank of North Dakota (1993–2000). He was elected to the Senate in 2010 after Senator Byron Dorgan retired.

221.name: Mark Kirk
221.alma_mater: Cornell University; London School of Economics; Georgetown University Law Center
221.biography: Mark Steven Kirk (born 1959) is a Republican politician and attorney who served as U.S. Representative for Illinois's 10th district (2001–2010) and U.S. Senator from Illinois (2010–2017). Often described as socially liberal and fiscally conservative, he also served in the U.S. Navy Reserve as an intelligence officer, reaching commander and retiring in 2013.

222.name: Roy Blunt
222.alma_mater: Southwest Baptist University; Southwest Missouri State University (now Missouri State University)
222.biography: Roy Dean Blunt (born 1950) is a Missouri Republican who served in Congress as U.S. Representative for Missouri’s 7th district (1997–2011) and U.S. Senator from Missouri (2011–2023). He previously was Missouri secretary of state (1985–1993). In the House he served as Republican whip (2003–2009) and later held leadership roles in the Senate Republican Conference.

223.name: Jerry Moran
223.alma_mater: University of Kansas; University of Kansas School of Law
223.biography: Gerald Wesley "Jerry" Moran (born 1954) is a Republican U.S. senator from Kansas, serving since 2011. Previously he represented Kansas’s 1st congressional district in the U.S. House (1997–2011) and served in the Kansas Senate (1989–1997), including as majority leader. Trained as a lawyer, he has also worked in private practice and in Kansas legal roles. He chaired the NRSC in the 113th Congress.

224.name: John Hoeven
224.biography: John Hoeven (born 1957) is a Republican U.S. senator from North Dakota, serving since 2011. He previously served as the 31st governor of North Dakota (2000–2010) and was president of the state-owned Bank of North Dakota (1993–2000). A former banker, he won election to the Senate in 2010 after Byron Dorgan retired.

225.name: Ron Johnson
225.biography: Ron Johnson (born 1955) is a Republican U.S. senator from Wisconsin, first elected in 2010 after defeating incumbent Russ Feingold. He served as a businessman and executive at a plastics manufacturing company in Oshkosh before entering politics. Johnson was reelected in 2016 (again defeating Feingold) and in 2022 (defeating Mandela Barnes). He is known for fiscal conservatism and opposition to expansive federal programs.

226.name: Joe Manchin
226.biography: Joseph A. Manchin III is a West Virginia politician and businessman. He served as West Virginia secretary of state (2001–2005) and governor (2005–2010). He won the 2010 special election to the U.S. Senate after Robert Byrd’s death and served from 2010 to 2025, becoming known as a centrist Democrat. In 2024 he left the Democratic Party and registered as an independent.

227.name: Richard Blumenthal
227.alma_mater: Harvard University; Trinity College, Cambridge; Yale Law School
227.biography: Richard Blumenthal (born 1946) is a Democratic politician and attorney serving as U.S. senator from Connecticut since 2011. He previously served in the Connecticut General Assembly, was U.S. attorney for the District of Connecticut (1977–1981), and was Connecticut attorney general (1991–2011). He is a Harvard graduate and Yale Law School alumnus, and served in the U.S. Marine Corps Reserve.

228.name: Scott Brown
228.alma_mater: Tufts University; Boston College Law School; Norfolk County Agricultural School
228.biography: Scott Brown is a Republican politician and diplomat who served as U.S. Senator from Massachusetts (2010–2013) after winning a 2010 special election. Previously he served in the Massachusetts House (1998–2004) and Senate (2004–2010). He lost reelection in 2012 to Elizabeth Warren and later ran unsuccessfully for Senate in New Hampshire in 2014. He was U.S. ambassador to New Zealand and Samoa (2017–2020).

229.name: Rob Portman
229.alma_mater: University of Michigan; University of Michigan Law School
229.biography: Rob Portman is a Republican politician and attorney who served as U.S. senator from Ohio (2011–2023). He previously represented Ohio’s 2nd district in the U.S. House (1993–2005), then served in the George W. Bush administration as U.S. trade representative (2005–2006) and OMB director (2006–2007).

230.name: Kelly Ayotte
230.alma_mater: Pennsylvania State University; Villanova University School of Law
230.biography: Kelly Ayotte is a Republican politician and attorney who served as U.S. senator from New Hampshire (2011–2017). Previously, she was New Hampshire attorney general (2004–2009), the first woman to hold that post, after serving as a prosecutor and deputy attorney general. Ayotte lost reelection to Democrat Maggie Hassan in 2016.

231.name: Patrick Toomey
231.biography: Patrick J. Toomey Jr. (born 1961) is a Republican politician and businessman who served as U.S. Senator from Pennsylvania (2011–2023). He previously represented Pennsylvania’s 15th congressional district in the U.S. House (1999–2005). A Harvard graduate and former Wall Street banker, he led the Club for Growth (2005–2009). He won election to the Senate in 2010 and was reelected in 2016.

232.name: Christopher Coons
232.alma_mater: Amherst College; Yale Divinity School; Yale Law School
232.biography: Christopher Andrew Coons is a Democratic politician and lawyer serving as U.S. Senator from Delaware since 2010. He previously was New Castle County executive (2005–2010) and served on the New Castle County Council, including as council president. Coons studied at Amherst College and earned graduate degrees from Yale Divinity School and Yale Law School, and worked in nonprofit, legal, and corporate roles before elected office.

233.name: Marco Rubio
233.alma_mater: University of Florida; University of Miami
233.biography: Marco Rubio (born 1971) is a Cuban American politician and attorney. He served on the West Miami City Commission, then in the Florida House (2000–2008), becoming speaker (2006–2008). Elected to the U.S. Senate from Florida in 2010, he served from 2011 to 2025. Rubio ran for the Republican presidential nomination in 2016 and became U.S. secretary of state in 2025.

234.name: Dean Heller
234.biography: Dean Arthur Heller (born 1960) is a Nevada Republican politician and businessman. He served as Nevada secretary of state (1995–2007), then represented Nevada’s 2nd congressional district in the U.S. House (2007–2011). Appointed to the U.S. Senate in 2011, he won election in 2012 and served until 2019, when he lost reelection to Jacky Rosen. He later ran unsuccessfully for Nevada governor in 2022.

235.name: Mike Lee
235.alma_mater: Brigham Young University; J. Reuben Clark Law School (BYU)
235.biography: Michael Shumway Lee is an American lawyer and Republican politician serving as U.S. senator from Utah since 2011. He previously clerked for federal judges including Samuel Alito, served as an assistant U.S. attorney (2002–2005), and was general counsel to Utah Governor Jon Huntsman Jr. (2005–2006). Elected in 2010, he became Utah’s senior senator in 2019.

236.name: Rand Paul
236.alma_mater: Baylor University; Duke University School of Medicine
236.biography: Randal Howard Paul (born 1963) is a Republican U.S. senator from Kentucky, first elected in 2010 and serving since 2011. A constitutional conservative associated with the Tea Party, he previously practiced as an ophthalmologist in Bowling Green, Kentucky. He ran for the 2016 Republican presidential nomination and has served on and chaired Senate committees, including Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

237.name: Tim Johnson
237.alma_mater: Texas State University; Georgetown University Law Center
237.biography: Tim Johnson was a Republican U.S. senator from Texas, appointed in 1996 to fill the seat vacated by Phil Gramm. He previously served in the Texas House and Texas Senate and worked as an attorney. He served in the U.S. Senate in the late 1990s and left office in 2002 after losing the Republican primary.

238.name: Joe Donnelly
238.biography: Joe Donnelly (born 1955) is an attorney and Indiana Democrat. He represented Indiana’s 2nd district in the U.S. House (2007–2013) and served as a U.S. senator from Indiana (2013–2019). After leaving the Senate, he was appointed U.S. ambassador to the Holy See (2022–2024) under President Joe Biden.

239.name: Heidi Heitkamp
239.biography: Heidi Heitkamp (born 1955) is a North Dakota attorney and politician. A member of the North Dakota Democratic–Nonpartisan League Party, she served as U.S. senator from North Dakota (2013–2019). She previously was North Dakota tax commissioner (1986–1992) and state attorney general (1992–2000). She ran unsuccessfully for governor in 2000 and won the 2012 Senate race, becoming the first woman elected to Congress from North Dakota.

240.name: William Cowan
240.biography: William “Mo” Cowan is an American politician and attorney who served as the appointed U.S. Senator from Massachusetts in 2013, filling the vacancy after John Kerry became Secretary of State. A Democrat, he did not run for election and was succeeded by Ed Markey. Cowan previously worked as chief of staff to Massachusetts Governor Deval Patrick and had a career in law and public administration.

241.name: John Walsh
241.alma_mater: University of Montana; United States Army War College
241.biography: John Edward Walsh (born 1960) is a Montana Democrat who served as U.S. Senator from Montana (2014–2015), appointed by Gov. Steve Bullock after Max Baucus resigned. He previously served as Montana’s lieutenant governor (2013–2014) and as adjutant general of the Montana National Guard (2008–2012), with a National Guard career reaching colonel/brigadier general. He ended his 2014 Senate campaign after plagiarism allegations.

242.name: John Boozman
242.alma_mater: University of Arkansas; Southern College of Optometry
242.biography: John Boozman (born 1950) is a Republican U.S. senator from Arkansas, serving since 2011. Previously, he represented Arkansas's 3rd congressional district in the U.S. House (2001–2011), first winning a 2001 special election. Trained as an optometrist, he co-founded a private clinic and did volunteer work for low-income families before entering politics.

243.name: Mazie Hirono
243.alma_mater: University of Hawaii at Manoa; Georgetown University Law Center
243.biography: Mazie Hirono (born 1947) is a Democratic U.S. senator from Hawaii, serving since 2013. She previously represented Hawaii’s 2nd congressional district in the U.S. House (2007–2013), served in the Hawaii House of Representatives (1981–1994), and was Hawaii’s lieutenant governor (1994–2002). She was the Democratic nominee for Hawaii governor in 2002, losing to Linda Lingle.

244.name: Timothy Kaine
244.biography: Timothy Michael Kaine (born 1958) is a Democratic U.S. senator from Virginia, elected in 2012 and serving since 2013. He previously served as Richmond city council member and mayor, then Virginia’s lieutenant governor (2002–2006) and governor (2006–2010). A lawyer and former law lecturer, he was the Democratic nominee for vice president in 2016 as Hillary Clinton’s running mate.

245.name: Angus King
245.alma_mater: Dartmouth College; University of Virginia School of Law
245.biography: Angus Stanley King Jr. (born 1944) is an attorney and politician serving as U.S. senator from Maine since 2013. An Independent who caucuses with Democrats, he previously served as Maine’s governor (1995–2003). Raised in Virginia, he moved to Maine after law school, worked in public broadcasting and business, and founded Northeast Energy Management before winning the governorship in 1994 and reelection in 1998.

246.name: Deb Fischer
246.biography: Deb Fischer (born 1951) is a Republican serving as U.S. senator from Nebraska since 2013. Raised on a ranch, she served on the Valentine Rural High School Board of Education (1990–2004) and in the Nebraska Legislature (2005–2013). She won election to the Senate in 2012, defeating Democrat Bob Kerrey, and was reelected in 2018 and 2024.

247.name: Roy Blunt
247.alma_mater: Southwest Baptist University; Southwest Missouri State University (now Missouri State University)
247.biography: Roy Blunt (born 1950) is a Missouri Republican who served in Congress as U.S. Representative for Missouri’s 7th district (1997–2011) and U.S. Senator from Missouri (2011–2023). He previously served as Missouri secretary of state (1985–1993). In the House he was Republican whip (2003–2009) and later held Senate Republican Conference leadership roles.

248.name: Ted Cruz
248.biography: Ted Cruz (born 1970) is a Republican U.S. Senator from Texas, first elected in 2012 and serving since 2013. An attorney, he was Solicitor General of Texas (2003–2008) and worked in private practice and government legal roles, including in the George W. Bush administration. In the Senate he is known for staunch conservative positions and a prominent role in the 2013 government shutdown.

249.name: Elizabeth Warren
249.alma_mater: University of Houston; Rutgers Law School
249.biography: Elizabeth Warren (born 1949) is a Democratic U.S. Senator from Massachusetts, serving since 2013. A former law professor specializing in bankruptcy, she gained national prominence as chair of the Congressional Oversight Panel for the 2008 financial crisis and as a leading advocate for consumer protection. She helped create the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau and ran for the 2020 Democratic presidential nomination.

250.name: Tammy Baldwin
250.alma_mater: Smith College; University of Wisconsin Law School
250.biography: Tammy Baldwin is a Democratic politician and lawyer serving as U.S. senator from Wisconsin since 2013. She previously served in the Wisconsin State Assembly (1993–1999) and represented Wisconsin’s 2nd congressional district in the U.S. House (1999–2013). Elected to the Senate in 2012, she was reelected in 2018 and 2024. She is the first openly lesbian person elected to the U.S. Senate.

251.name: Brian Schatz
251.biography: Brian Emanuel Schatz (born 1972) is a progressive Democrat serving as U.S. senator from Hawaii since 2012, appointed after Daniel Inouye’s death and later elected in 2014. He previously served in the Hawaii House of Representatives (1998–2006), chaired the Democratic Party of Hawaii (2008–2010), worked as CEO of Helping Hands Hawaii, and was Hawaii’s lieutenant governor (2010–2012).

252.name: Martin Heinrich
252.biography: Martin Heinrich (born 1971) is a Democratic U.S. Senator from New Mexico, elected in 2012 and serving since 2013. He previously represented New Mexico’s 1st congressional district in the U.S. House (2009–2013) and served on the Albuquerque City Council (2004–2008). Born in Fallon, Nevada, he built his political career in Albuquerque after earning a B.S. in mechanical engineering from the University of New Mexico.

253.name: Edward Markey
253.alma_mater: Boston College; Boston College Law School
253.biography: Edward J. Markey (born 1946) is a Democrat serving as U.S. senator from Massachusetts since 2013, elected in a special election to succeed John Kerry and later to full terms. He previously served in the Massachusetts House (1973–1976) and represented Massachusetts in the U.S. House (1976–2013), becoming the longtime dean of the state’s congressional delegation.

254.name: Cory Booker
254.alma_mater: Stanford University; University of Oxford (The Queen's College); Yale Law School
254.biography: Cory Anthony Booker (born 1969) is a Democratic U.S. senator from New Jersey, first elected in a 2013 special election and later winning full terms. He previously served on the Newark Municipal Council (1998–2002) and as mayor of Newark (2006–2013). A former Rhodes Scholar, he is the first African American to represent New Jersey in the U.S. Senate.

255.name: Tim Scott
255.biography: Tim Scott (born 1965) is a Republican U.S. senator from South Carolina, appointed in 2013 after Jim DeMint resigned and later elected in 2014 and 2016. He previously served on the Charleston County Council (1995–2009), in the South Carolina House (2009–2011), and as U.S. representative for South Carolina’s 1st district (2011–2013). He worked in insurance and financial services.

256.name: Jeffrey Chiesa
256.biography: Jeffrey Scott Chiesa is an American lawyer and Republican politician. He served as Chief Counsel to New Jersey Governor Chris Christie, then as New Jersey Attorney General (2012–2013). Christie appointed him to the U.S. Senate after Senator Frank Lautenberg’s death; Chiesa served as U.S. Senator from New Jersey from June to October 2013 and did not run in the special election won by Cory Booker.

257.name: Jeff Flake
257.biography: Jeff Flake (born 1962) is a Republican politician who served as U.S. senator from Arizona (2013–2019) after representing Arizona in the U.S. House (2001–2013). He previously led the Goldwater Institute and later served as U.S. ambassador to Turkey (2022–2024). He earned a BA in international relations and an MA in political science from Brigham Young University.

258.name: Christopher Murphy
258.alma_mater: Williams College; University of Connecticut School of Law
258.biography: Christopher Scott Murphy (born 1973) is a Democratic U.S. senator from Connecticut, serving since 2013. He previously represented Connecticut’s 5th district in the U.S. House (2007–2013). Earlier, he served in the Connecticut House (1999–2003) and Connecticut Senate (2003–2007). Elected to the U.S. Senate in 2012 after Joe Lieberman’s retirement, he defeated Linda McMahon and was reelected in 2018 and 2024.

259.name: Tammy Baldwin
259.alma_mater: Smith College; University of Wisconsin Law School
259.biography: Tammy Baldwin (born 1962) is a Democratic U.S. Senator from Wisconsin, serving since 2013. She previously served in the Wisconsin State Assembly (1993–1999) and represented Wisconsin’s 2nd congressional district in the U.S. House (1999–2013). A lawyer by training, she is a Smith College and University of Wisconsin Law School graduate. She was elected to the Senate in 2012 and reelected in 2018 and 2024.

260.name: David Perdue
260.biography: David Perdue (born 1949) is a Republican businessman and politician who served as U.S. Senator from Georgia from 2015 to 2021. A former executive and CEO at companies including Reebok and Dollar General, he won election in 2014 and lost reelection to Jon Ossoff in a 2021 runoff. He later ran unsuccessfully for Georgia governor in 2022.

261.name: Joni Ernst
261.biography: Joni Ernst is a Republican politician and retired Army National Guard lieutenant colonel serving as U.S. senator from Iowa since 2015. She previously served as Montgomery County auditor (2005–2011) and in the Iowa State Senate (2011–2014). Elected to the U.S. Senate in 2014 and reelected in 2020, she has held GOP leadership roles, including chair of the Senate Republican Policy Committee (2023–2025).

262.name: Thom Tillis
262.biography: Thomas Roland Tillis (born 1960) is a Republican U.S. senator from North Carolina, first elected in 2014 and reelected in 2020. Before Congress he served in the North Carolina House of Representatives (2007–2015) and was Speaker of the House (2011–2015). A former businessman, he has served on several Senate committees, including Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

263.name: Ben Sasse
263.alma_mater: Harvard University; St. John's College; Yale University
263.biography: Benjamin Eric Sasse served as a U.S. Senator from Nebraska (2015–2023). An academic administrator, he was president of Midland University (2010–2015) and later became president of the University of Florida, prompting his Senate resignation. He also served in the George W. Bush administration as an assistant secretary at the Department of Health and Human Services. A Republican, he was a prominent critic of Donald Trump.

264.name: Shelley Capito
264.biography: Shelley Moore Capito (born 1953) is a Republican serving as U.S. senator from West Virginia since 2015, the first woman elected to the Senate from the state. She previously represented West Virginia’s 2nd congressional district in the U.S. House from 2001 to 2015. The daughter of former governor and congressman Arch A. Moore Jr., she was elected to the Senate in 2014 and reelected in 2020.

265.name: Bill Cassidy
265.alma_mater: Louisiana State University; LSU School of Medicine
265.biography: Bill Cassidy is a Republican politician and physician who has served as U.S. senator from Louisiana since 2015. He previously represented Louisiana’s 6th congressional district in the U.S. House (2009–2015) and served in the Louisiana State Senate (2006–2009). A gastroenterologist, he was born in Highland Park, Illinois, and won the 2014 Senate race, defeating incumbent Mary Landrieu; he was reelected in 2020.

266.name: James Lankford
266.alma_mater: University of Texas at Austin; Southwestern Baptist Theological Seminary
266.biography: James Paul Lankford (born 1968) is a Republican serving as Oklahoma’s senior U.S. senator since 2015. An ordained Southern Baptist minister, he previously led the Falls Creek Baptist Conference Center (1996–2009). Lankford represented Oklahoma’s 5th congressional district in the U.S. House from 2011 to 2015. He won a 2014 special Senate election after Tom Coburn’s resignation and was reelected in 2016 and 2022.

267.name: Steve Daines
267.biography: Steve Daines is a Republican politician and former corporate executive. He represented Montana’s at-large U.S. House seat from 2013 to 2015, then was elected U.S. senator from Montana in 2014 and took office in 2015. A longtime business leader, he worked at Procter & Gamble and RightNow Technologies. He won reelection to the Senate in 2020.

268.name: Tom Cotton
268.biography: Thomas Bryant Cotton (born 1977) is a Republican U.S. senator from Arkansas, serving since 2015. A former U.S. Army officer and Iraq/Afghanistan veteran, he previously represented Arkansas's 4th congressional district in the U.S. House (2013–2015). Elected to the Senate in 2014, he is known for hawkish national-security and foreign-policy positions.

269.name: Mike Rounds
269.biography: Mike Rounds (born 1954) is a Republican U.S. senator from South Dakota, serving since 2015. He previously served in the South Dakota Senate (1991–2001) and was the 31st governor of South Dakota (2003–2011). A businessman, he was elected to the U.S. Senate in 2014, succeeding retiring Democrat Tim Johnson.

270.name: Daniel Sullivan
270.alma_mater: Georgetown University; Georgetown University Law Center; Harvard Kennedy School
270.biography: Daniel Scott Sullivan is a Republican U.S. Senator from Alaska, elected in 2014. A lawyer and former U.S. Marine Corps officer, he served as Alaska Attorney General (2009–2010), U.S. Assistant Secretary of State for Economic, Energy and Business Affairs (2013–2014), and Alaska Commissioner of Natural Resources (2010–2013).

271.name: Gary Peters
271.alma_mater: Alma College; Michigan State University; Wayne State University; University of Detroit Mercy
271.biography: Gary Charles Peters (born 1958) is a Democratic U.S. senator from Michigan, serving since 2015. He previously represented Michigan in the U.S. House (2009–2015) and served in the Michigan Senate (1995–2002) and on the Rochester Hills City Council. A former Navy Reserve officer and investment advisor, he was the 2002 Democratic nominee for Michigan attorney general and later led the Michigan Lottery.

272.name: Jerry Moran
272.alma_mater: University of Kansas; University of Kansas School of Law
272.biography: Jerry Moran (born 1954) is a Republican U.S. Senator from Kansas, serving since 2011. Previously he represented Kansas’s 1st congressional district in the U.S. House (1997–2011) and served in the Kansas Senate (1989–1997), including as majority leader. A lawyer, he also worked in private practice and held state legal roles in Kansas.

273.name: Cory Gardner
273.biography: Cory Scott Gardner (born 1974) is a Republican politician and attorney who served as U.S. Senator from Colorado (2015–2021). He previously represented Colorado’s 4th district in the U.S. House (2011–2015) and served in the Colorado House of Representatives (2005–2011). Elected to the Senate in 2014, he chaired the National Republican Senatorial Committee (2017–2019) and lost reelection in 2020.

274.name: Cory Booker
274.alma_mater: Stanford University; University of Oxford (Queen's College); Yale Law School
274.biography: Cory Anthony Booker (born 1969) is a Democratic U.S. senator from New Jersey, first elected in a 2013 special election and later reelected. He previously served on the Newark Municipal Council (1998–2002) and as mayor of Newark (2006–2013). A Rhodes Scholar, he studied at Stanford, Oxford, and Yale Law. He is New Jersey’s first African-American U.S. senator.

275.name: Chris Van Hollen
275.alma_mater: Swarthmore College; Harvard University; Georgetown University Law Center
275.biography: Chris Van Hollen is a Democratic politician and attorney serving as U.S. Senator from Maryland since 2017. He previously represented Maryland’s 8th congressional district in the U.S. House (2003–2017) and served in the Maryland State Senate (1995–2003). In House leadership, he chaired the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee and served on budget-related roles.

276.name: Luther Strange
276.biography: Luther Strange is an American lawyer and Republican politician. He served as Alabama Attorney General (2011–2017) after defeating incumbent Troy King in the GOP primary. In 2017 he was appointed to the U.S. Senate to fill the vacancy left when Jeff Sessions became U.S. Attorney General, serving until 2018. He previously ran for Alabama lieutenant governor in 2006, losing the general election.

277.name: John Kennedy
277.biography: John Neely Kennedy is a Republican U.S. Senator from Louisiana, elected in 2016 and taking office in 2017. Previously he served as Louisiana state treasurer (2000–2017) and earlier held posts in state government and private law practice. He switched from Democrat to Republican in 2007. He is known for fiscal-conservative positions and prominent questioning in Senate hearings.

278.name: Catherine Cortez Masto
278.alma_mater: University of Nevada, Reno; Gonzaga University School of Law
278.biography: Catherine Cortez Masto is a Democratic U.S. senator from Nevada, serving since 2017. Trained as a lawyer, she worked in civil practice and as a federal prosecutor. She was Nevada’s attorney general from 2007 to 2015 and, in 2016, won election to the Senate to succeed Harry Reid, becoming the first woman and first Latina elected to represent Nevada in the Senate.

279.name: Kamala Harris
279.alma_mater: Howard University; University of California, Hastings College of the Law
279.biography: Kamala Devi Harris (born 1964) is a Democrat who served as U.S. Senator from California (2017–2021). Previously she was California attorney general (2011–2017) and San Francisco district attorney (2004–2011). In the Senate she served on major committees including Judiciary and Intelligence. Harris became vice president of the United States (2021–2025) under President Joe Biden.

280.name: Tammy Duckworth
280.alma_mater: University of Hawaii at Manoa; George Washington University; Northern Illinois University
280.biography: Tammy Duckworth is a Democratic U.S. Senator from Illinois (2017–present) and an Army National Guard veteran. She previously represented Illinois’s 8th congressional district in the U.S. House (2013–2017). A former Army helicopter pilot, she was severely wounded in Iraq in 2004, losing both legs and partial use of her right arm. She later served in veterans and Illinois state roles.

281.name: Maggie Hassan
281.alma_mater: Brown University; Northeastern University School of Law
281.biography: Maggie Hassan (born 1958) is a Democratic U.S. Senator from New Hampshire, serving since 2017. An attorney, she previously served in the New Hampshire Senate (2005–2010) and was the 81st governor of New Hampshire (2013–2017). She worked in private practice and as associate general counsel for Brigham and Women’s Hospital before her elected career.

282.name: Todd Young
282.alma_mater: United States Naval Academy; University of Chicago; Indiana University Bloomington
282.biography: Todd Young (born 1972) is a Republican U.S. senator from Indiana, serving since 2017. A former U.S. Navy officer, he later worked as an attorney. Young represented Indiana’s 9th congressional district in the U.S. House from 2011 to 2017. He won election to the Senate in 2016, succeeding Dan Coats, became Indiana’s senior senator in 2019, chaired the NRSC (2019–2021), and was reelected in 2022.
